topics.rationale: This is where the search <input>, the Search <button>, and the Enter-keydown listener are built and wired. The task requires: removing the Search button, adding a magnifying-glass icon and an x-icon-button inside the input (aligned right), wiring a debounced (200ms) 'input' event to trigger instant search, wiring the x-button click and 'esc' keydown to clear the input and navigate back to the browse route, and updating the DomRefs interface in context.ts to expose any new elements (e.g. clear button). Existing tests for the Search button click and Enter key will need updating.

The task requires a loading spinner to appear below the streamed results while a search is running. The orchestrator (render-orchestrator.ts) currently clears results and awaits a single fetch before delegating to render-search; a spinner must be shown during that in-flight window and removed when results arrive, respecting the existing render-race token guard. render-search.ts builds the results table where the spinner sits. The api.ts SearchResult type defines whether results arrive in one batch (affecting how 'streamed as they appear' is achieved). Tests assert render dispatch and will need spinner assertions.

topics.topic: CSS styling for in-input icons & spinner
topics.rationale: The magnifying glass and x-icon must be positioned inside the right side of the search text input (overlaid), and the input needs right-padding so typed text never underlaps the icons. A loading-spinner needs CSS (animation, sizing, placement below results). app.css currently styles .toolbar input with margin-left:auto and no icon overlays, so new positioning rules and a spinner keyframe are required. The CSS tests assert the stylesheet structure and will need updating for the new icon/spinner rules.

topics.topic: Bootstrap Icons assets & glyph helper convention
topics.rationale: The icons (magnifying glass 'bi-search', close 'bi-x-lg', and a spinner glyph such as 'bi-arrow-repeat'/'bi-loader') are provided by the Bootstrap Icons font already loaded in index.html. This topic confirms which icon names are available in the vendored bootstrap-icons.css, how the asset is copied into wwwroot/dist (scripts/copy-assets.mjs), and the established makeIcon helper in dom-builders.ts that builds `<i class='bi bi-<name>'>` elements — the convention to follow when constructing the toolbar icons and spinner.

output.research:
The four scouting reports together provide a complete picture of the codebase areas affected by the task. 

**Current state (summarized):** The toolbar (built in `app.ts:66-72`) contains `searchInput` (text input with placeholder 'Search...'), `searchBtn` (button.btn 'Search'), breadcrumb, and upload label appended to a flex toolbar. The search button click and Enter key on the input both call `doSearch()` from `toolbar-handlers.ts`, which reads the input value, trims it, and navigates to either a search hash or browse hash (if empty). The render lifecycle (`render-orchestrator.ts`) clears `resultsEl.innerHTML`, awaits the API call, checks a race token, then delegates to `renderSearch`/`renderBrowse`. There is no debounce utility, no clear-search handler, no in-input icons, no spinner element or CSS animation. Bootstrap Icons (v^1.13.1, 2080 icons) are loaded via `wwwroot/index.html` and the `makeIcon()` helper in `dom-builders.ts` creates `<i class="bi bi-{name}">` elements. The API client (`api.ts`) returns a single `Promise<SearchResult>` with all results in one batch — no streaming support on client or server.

**Changes required:**
1. **DOM (app.ts):** Remove `searchBtn` creation and append. Wrap `searchInput` in a container div (e.g. `.search-wrapper`) with `position:relative`. Add a magnifying glass icon (`makeIcon('search')`) visible when input is idle, and an X icon button (`makeIcon('x-lg')` wrapped in a `<button>`) visible when input has text, both absolutely positioned/right-aligned inside the wrapper. The wrapper (not the input) gets `margin-left:auto` for toolbar alignment.

2. **Event wiring (app.ts):** Add `input` event listener with 200ms debounce on `searchInput` → calls a debounced `doSearch()`. Add click listener on X button → clears input and navigates to browse hash for current path. Add Escape key handler on `searchInput` keydown → same clear behavior (with `stopPropagation()` to prevent native `<dialog>` closing). Remove searchBtn click listener. Keep or remove Enter handler (redundant with debounced input, but UX-friendly to keep).

3. **Handler logic (toolbar-handlers.ts):** Create a `clearSearch()` function that resets `searchInput.value` and navigates to `toBrowseHash(normalizedPath)`. Create a debounce wrapper (inline or shared utility). The existing `doSearch()` can remain the core navigation logic; it is called by the debounced input handler.

4. **Context (context.ts):** Add `searchClearBtn: HTMLElement` to `DomRefs` interface, corresponding `let` variable, `setRefs` assignment, and getter function so the clear handler can reference the X button element (e.g., to toggle its visibility).

5. **Render lifecycle (render-orchestrator.ts):** After `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''` and before `await getApi().search(...)`, insert a spinner element (e.g. `<i class="bi bi-arrow-repeat spinning">`) into `resultsEl` (or after the table). After the race-token check and `renderSearch(result)`, remove the spinner. The race-token guard must also guard spinner show/hide to prevent superseded renders from removing a newer render's spinner. For browse renders, no spinner is needed.

6. **CSS (app.css):** Add `.search-wrapper` with `position:relative; display:inline-flex; align-items:center`. Add `.search-wrapper .search-icon` and `.search-wrapper .clear-btn` positioned absolutely with `right: 4px` and `cursor:pointer`. Add right-padding to the input to avoid text underlap. Toggle X button visibility via a class (e.g. `.has-text .clear-btn` vs `.search-wrapper:not(.has-text) .search-icon`) or use `:placeholder-shown` sibling selectors. Add `@keyframes spin { to { transform: rotate(360deg) } }` and a `.spinning { animation: spin 1s linear infinite }` class for the spinner glyph (`bi-arrow-repeat`).

7. **Tests:** Update `SetupCtx` in `test-helpers.ts` to remove `searchBtn` and add relevant new selectors. Update `setup()` to remove `button('Search')` lookup. Update `start-app.test.ts` to remove/update the 'search text input with Search button' test and add assertions for the icon elements. Update `toolbar-handlers.test.ts` to replace Search-button-click tests with debounced-input tests, X-button click tests, and Escape key tests. Update render lifecycle tests to assert spinner presence during in-flight fetches (using held-fetch patterns from race-token tests).

8. **Streaming consideration:** The current API (`GET /api/files/search`) returns all results as a single JSON batch. The task says results are 'streamed as they appear.' The planner must decide whether to (a) implement actual streaming by changing the server to NDJSON/chunked response and the client to ReadableStream parsing with incremental row appending, or (b) keep the batch approach with the spinner showing during the single fetch and all results appearing at once. The reports flag this as an open concern but classify deeper API investigation as out of scope for the toolbar/render topics.

output.feedback: The plan is comprehensive, logically sound, and directly implementable. All 8 requirements from the task description map to planned tasks, the dependency ordering is valid with no circular dependencies, and every task provides concrete, unambiguous implementation instructions with exact code snippets. The design decisions (CSS-only visibility toggling instead of JS refs, inline debounce instead of shared utility, batch API instead of streaming) are all well-justified and consistent with the research context. Key correctness claims were verified: renderSearch/renderBrowse both clear innerHTML before building tables (confirming the spinner will be naturally removed), all toolbar-handlers.ts imports needed by clearSearch() already exist, and the race-token mechanism correctly guards against superseded spinner renders.
suggestions: Task 7, Escape propagation test: The `propagationStopped` variable (initialized to `false`, set to `false` in the listener, never asserted) is dead code with a logic inversion bug. It should be removed entirely since the spy assertion is sufficient, or corrected to `let propagationStopped = true` with an `expect(propagationStopped).toBe(true)` assertion. This doesn't affect test correctness but is misleading.

Task 8, first spinner test: The comment 'settle the initial browse render' is inaccurate — the setup hash is a search hash, so it's settling the initial search render. Consider correcting to 'settle the initial render' for clarity.

Task 4, debounce timer cleanup: Consider adding cleanup of the debounce timer in the `afterEach` or when the dialog closes. If the dialog is closed while a debounce timer is pending, the timer's callback will still fire and call `doSearch()` after 200ms. While this is harmless (navigating to browse when already on browse is a no-op in happy-dom), it could cause unexpected behavior in edge cases. This is a minor robustness improvement, not a correctness issue.

The implementation matches the task description exactly. The spinner is inserted between `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''` and the `try` block, guarded by `if (route.view === 'search')`. Browse routes correctly receive no spinner. No removal logic was added, as specified — the comment explains why none is needed (renderSearch clears the container; the error handler uses textContent). The race-token mechanism, init() function, and error handling are all untouched. The inline comment is well-placed and describes current behavior without referencing past implementations. No dead code, unused variables, or scope expansion detected.

3. Combined `.search-icon, .clear-btn` positioning rule (`position:absolute; right:6px; color:#9a9a9a;`); separate `.search-icon { pointer-events:none; }`; full `.clear-btn` chromeless-button styling; `.clear-btn:hover { color:#e6e6e6; }`. output.feedback:
COMPLETION: All specified tests are present at the end of the file. The git diff shows ONLY additions (lines 598+); no existing test was modified, satisfying the 'Do NOT modify any existing tests' constraint. All 9 new tests pass (suite went from 41→50 tests, 40→49 passing).

NECESSARY DEVIATIONS (justified): Two test bodies deviate from the literal spec code, and both deviations are correct engineering:
(1) '.search-wrapper .clear-btn is hidden by default' — the spec'd `ruleDeclarations(/\.search-wrapper \.clear-btn(?![\w[#.:])/)` returns the FIRST matching rule, which is the combined `.search-icon, .clear-btn { position:absolute; ... }` rule, NOT the default-hidden `display:none` rule. The spec'd assertion would have failed. The implementation correctly verifies against the raw CSS string instead, with a clear comment explaining why.
(2) 'the search icon and clear button are absolutely positioned right-aligned' — the spec'd `ruleDeclarations(/\.search-wrapper \.search-icon/)` returns the `pointer-events:none` rule (the combined positioning rule's selector ends with `, .clear-btn {`, so the `\s*\{` anchor doesn't match `.search-icon` there), which has no `position:absolute`. The implementation correctly switches to `CSS.toMatch(...)` against the raw stylesheet, again with a clear explanatory comment.

These deviations honor the task's INTENT (pin the search-wrapper/icon/spinner contract via `ruleDeclarations`) while fixing two latent bugs in the spec'd code. They do not expand scope.

PATTERNS/CLEANLINESS: The new code follows existing conventions (describe/it/expect, the `ruleDeclarations` helper, rich explanatory comments matching the file's established style). No dead code, no unused imports, no debug statements, no magic literals needing constants. The added block-header comment clearly frames the new tests as a 'companion contract' restatement.

The pre-existing failing test (`.row-menu-btn[aria-expanded="true"]` mismatch — CSS uses single quotes, test regex uses double quotes) was failing BEFORE this task and is out of scope (task forbids modifying existing tests); flagged below as WONTFIX context only.

output.feedback: The implementation faithfully applies the task spec: (1) `vi` is imported from vitest; (2) all 5 old search-button-click tests are removed; (3) all 8 new tests (debounced input, keystroke reset, trimming, empty-query clear, X-button clear, Escape clear, Escape stopPropagation, Enter bypass) are present and match the spec verbatim; (4) the `describe('upload')` and `describe('pickAndUploadInto')` blocks are unchanged. Verification: `npx vitest run src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts` reports 23 passed / 23 total. One trivial observation (not a blocker since it is verbatim from the spec): in the 'Escape key stops propagation' test, the local `propagationStopped` variable is initialized to false and only ever reassigned to false inside the parent's keydown listener; it is never asserted on — the actual verification is via `vi.spyOn(event, 'stopPropagation')`. This is dead code in the strict sense, but altering it would deviate from the prescribed source.

Implementation is complete and compliant with the task description. All three specified changes are present and match the spec verbatim:

1. The search input test was replaced with 'renders a search text input inside a .search-wrapper with a magnifying glass icon and a clear button' — destructures searchWrapper/searchIcon/searchClearBtn from setup(), asserts the input type/placeholder, the wrapper class+containment, the magnifying-glass icon (bi-search + search-icon), the clear button (clear-btn class, 'Clear search' aria-label, bi-x-lg icon). All assertions match the spec exactly.

2. The new 'does NOT render a Search button' test was added immediately after, asserting zero buttons whose trimmed textContent is 'Search'. Matches the spec exactly.

output.notApplicableReason: This changeset has no security-relevant surface. All 14 changed files are frontend UI/UX additions to a TypeScript SPA: a search icon, a clear-search button, a spinner shown during search fetches, and a 200ms input debounce — plus their unit tests, CSS, and an ESLint passthrough config. I verified via git diff (main...HEAD) that there are NO changes to server-side C# code, Controllers, Services, Program.cs, appsettings, package.json/lock, dependencies, authentication/authorization, crypto, or network transport. The DOM is built exclusively with safe APIs (document.createElement, element.append, fixed className strings, and textContent for errors), with the only innerHTML use being `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''` (a safe clear). User input (the search query) flows only into the pre-existing hash-route navigation path, unchanged by this diff, and the debounce merely reduces request frequency. I also reviewed the committed `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak`: it is a plain duplicate of source code with no secrets, credentials, or sensitive data, so it is a repository-hygiene issue rather than a security vulnerability. There is nothing here for a security review to flag.
output.summary: Reviewed the full diff (main...HEAD): all changes are frontend-only UI work in the SPA (search icon, clear button, spinner, input debounce) plus tests, CSS, and an ESLint passthrough config. No server-side, auth, crypto, secret, dependency, or untrusted-input-to-query changes were introduced; DOM is constructed with safe APIs and the debounce reduces request load. No security-relevant surface exists, so the security dimension is not applicable to this changeset. Findings: none.

Reviewed the runtime-relevant changes: the search-loading spinner in src/app/render-orchestrator.ts (creates 2 lightweight DOM elements per search render — O(1), negligible), the debounced search input and search-wrapper DOM construction in src/app/app.ts, the new clearSearch() in src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts, and the CSS additions in src/app.css. Overall the changes are efficiency-positive: debouncing cuts redundant API calls on every keystroke, the clear-button visibility is toggled purely via CSS :placeholder-shown (no JS work per keystroke), and the spinner animation is transform-based (GPU-accelerated) and removed from the DOM once results render. The leftover src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak is a code-hygiene issue, not a runtime efficiency concern (the tsconfig `src/**/*.ts` glob does not match a `.bak` extension, so it is not compiled or bundled). I found one minor redundancy: a pending debounce timer is not cleared when Enter or Escape acts immediately, causing one redundant doSearch()/navigate() call shortly after.

findings.title: Enter/Escape do not cancel the pending search debounce timer, causing a redundant doSearch
findings.description: The new `input` listener schedules a 200ms debounce timer that calls `doSearch()`. The `keydown` handler calls `doSearch()` immediately on Enter and `clearSearch()` on Escape, but neither branch clears `searchDebounceTimer`. So when a user types characters (arming the debounce) and then presses Enter or Escape before the 200ms elapses, the immediate action runs AND the still-pending debounce timer fires ~200ms later, invoking `doSearch()` a second time with the same (now-empty or unchanged) value. The second call is largely benign — `navigate()` to an unchanged hash does not dispatch `hashchange` and so triggers no re-render — but it is genuinely redundant work (DOM reads, route normalization, and a navigation call) on a user-interactive hot path. Clearing the timer in the Enter and Escape branches removes the redundant invocation.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/app.ts, the `keydown` listener on `searchInput` calls `doSearch()` on Enter and `clearSearch()` on Escape without cancelling the pending debounce timer (`searchDebounceTimer`) that the `input` listener arms. Update the `keydown` handler so that, in BOTH the Enter branch and the Escape branch, it first cancels any pending debounce timer before acting: i.e. `if (searchDebounceTimer !== null) { clearTimeout(searchDebounceTimer); searchDebounceTimer = null; }`. This prevents the debounce timer from firing ~200ms later and invoking `doSearch()` a second time (redundant DOM reads, route normalization, and navigation) after the immediate Enter/Escape action has already been taken. Do not change the debounce logic in the `input` listener itself, and preserve the existing `event.stopPropagation()` call in the Escape branch. Keep all other behavior identical.

findings.title: Enter/Escape do not cancel the pending search debounce timer, causing a redundant doSearch
findings.description: The new `input` listener schedules a 200ms debounce timer that calls `doSearch()`. The `keydown` handler calls `doSearch()` immediately on Enter and `clearSearch()` on Escape, but neither branch clears `searchDebounceTimer`. So when a user types characters (arming the debounce) and then presses Enter or Escape before the 200ms elapses, the immediate action runs AND the still-pending debounce timer fires ~200ms later, invoking `doSearch()` a second time with the same (now-empty or unchanged) value. The second call is largely benign — `navigate()` to an unchanged hash does not dispatch `hashchange` and so triggers no re-render — but it is genuinely redundant work (DOM reads, route normalization, and a navigation call) on a user-interactive hot path. Clearing the timer in the Enter and Escape branches removes the redundant invocation.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/app.ts, the `keydown` listener on `searchInput` calls `doSearch()` on Enter and `clearSearch()` on Escape without cancelling the pending debounce timer (`searchDebounceTimer`) that the `input` listener arms. Update the `keydown` handler so that, in BOTH the Enter branch and the Escape branch, it first cancels any pending debounce timer before acting: i.e. `if (searchDebounceTimer !== null) { clearTimeout(searchDebounceTimer); searchDebounceTimer = null; }`. This prevents the debounce timer from firing ~200ms later and invoking `doSearch()` a second time (redundant DOM reads, route normalization, and navigation) after the immediate Enter/Escape action has already been taken. Do not change the debounce logic in the `input` listener itself, and preserve the existing `event.stopPropagation()` call in the Escape branch. Keep all other behavior identical.

output.summary: Reviewed the entire changeset (src/app.css, src/app.ts, src/app/dom-builders.ts, render-browse.ts, render-orchestrator.ts, toolbar-handlers.ts). The work is overwhelmingly UI-facing: a new search-wrapper with overlaid icon + CSS-driven clear button, debounced instant search, a loading spinner, Bootstrap Icons on every menu item, a right-click current-directory context menu, and whole-row click-to-browse. Visual styling and interaction polish are generally solid (correct box-sizing fix on menu anchors, pointer cursors on navigable rows, clamped menu positioning, focus-within reflection on the upload label). The main gaps are accessibility: the search input has no real accessible name (placeholder only), decorative icons are not hidden from AT, the spinner is not announced, the row-menu advertises menu semantics it does not implement, and a couple of small contrast/touch-target/copy issues.

findings.title: Search input is labeled only by its placeholder, with no accessible name
findings.description: The toolbar search input is created with `type='text'` and `placeholder='Search...'` but no `aria-label` and no associated `<label>`. A placeholder is not an accessible name: it vanishes the moment the user types, has low contrast, and is an unreliable/last-resort source in the accessible-name computation. Notably the upload input right next to it correctly gets `uploadInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Upload files')`, so this is an inconsistency. Screen-reader users get either no name or a fragile one. WCAG 1.3.1 (Info and Relationships) and 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value) require a programmatically determinable name. Add an `aria-label` (the wrapper holds no visible text label, so `aria-label` is the right mechanism, matching the upload input).
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.ts, in startApp where the search input is built (around the lines `const searchInput = document.createElement('input'); searchInput.type = 'text'; searchInput.placeholder = 'Search...';`), add `searchInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Search files');` immediately after the placeholder is set. Do not remove the placeholder (it doubles as the empty-state hint for sighted users and drives the `:placeholder-shown` clear-button toggle). Verify no existing test asserts the absence of an aria-label; if any does, update it to expect 'Search files'.

findings.title: Decorative Bootstrap-Icons <i> glyphs are not hidden from assistive tech
findings.description: Every icon added in this changeset — the in-input search glyph, the clear button's `bi-x-lg`, the close button's `bi-x-lg`, the spinner's `bi-arrow-repeat`, and all the menu-item glyphs produced by `makeIcon` ('upload', 'download', 'trash', 'arrows-move', 'files', 'folder-plus') — are `<i>` elements with no `aria-hidden`. Bootstrap Icons render their glyph via a CSS `::before { content: '\f...' }` rule, and some screen readers announce generated content / private-use-area code points as stray noise. Because each icon is paired with a visible text label (or, for the clear/close buttons, an `aria-label`), the icon is purely decorative and must be hidden from AT per WCAG 1.3.1. The central fix is in `makeIcon` (covers all menu-item icons); the few icons created inline in app.ts and render-orchestrator.ts must be patched separately.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/dom-builders.ts, in the `makeIcon(name)` function, before returning the element add `icon.setAttribute('aria-hidden', 'true');`. Then in src/app.ts set the same attribute on `searchIcon` (the `bi bi-search search-icon` element) and on `clearIcon` (the `bi bi-x-lg` inside the clear button) right after each is created; also set it on `closeIcon` (the dialog close button's `bi bi-x-lg`). In src/app/render-orchestrator.ts set `aria-hidden='true'` on the spinner icon (`spinnerIcon`, class `bi bi-arrow-repeat spinning`). Run the existing dom-builders / start-app / render-orchestration tests; none should assert the absence of aria-hidden, but if a snapshot-like assertion fails, update it.

findings.title: Search loading spinner is visual-only; screen readers get no 'loading' announcement
findings.description: When a search is in flight, sighted users see a centered spinning `bi-arrow-repeat`, but the spinner container is a plain `<div>` with no `role='status'` and no `aria-live` region, so assistive-tech users receive no feedback that work is happening (and the previous results simply vanish with no explanation). WCAG 4.1.3 (Status Messages) calls for status messages to be announced programmatically. Mark the spinner wrapper as a polite live region so its presence is announced when it appears.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/render-orchestrator.ts, where the spinner is constructed (the `if (route.view === 'search')` block that builds `spinnerWrap` and `spinnerIcon`), add `spinnerWrap.setAttribute('role', 'status');` and `spinnerWrap.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Loading search results');` after creating `spinnerWrap`. Keep the spinner icon itself hidden from AT per the decorative-icons finding (it already has no text content). Do not change the visual styling.

findings.title: Row menu advertises `aria-haspopup='true'` (menu) but does not implement the menu pattern
findings.description: The per-row ⋮ button sets `aria-haspopup='true'` (which per spec is equivalent to `aria-haspopup='menu'`) and toggles `aria-expanded`, signaling to AT users that activating it opens a menu widget with arrow-key item navigation, Home/End, and focus moving to the first item on open. The actual dropdown is a plain `<div class='row-menu'>` containing `<button class='btn'>` / `<a class='btn'>` items with no `role='menu'`/`role='menuitem'`, no focus move into the menu on open, and no arrow-key handling — only Tab/Shift+Tab and Escape work. This expectation mismatch is confusing for screen-reader users. Either complete the menu semantics or downgrade to a plain disclosure. The lower-risk, self-contained fix is to complete the semantics by adding the roles.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/dom-builders.ts `createMenu` (which builds the shared `.row-menu` element), set `menu.setAttribute('role', 'menu')` on the created `menu` div, and set `role='menuitem'` on every element in the passed-in `items` array (iterate and set the attribute, skipping any element that already has a role). Then in `makeRowMenu`'s `openMenu`/click handler, after the menu is shown, focus the first item: add `(items[0] as HTMLElement)?.focus()` is not available here, so instead in `createMenu`'s `openMenu`, after positioning, call `(menu.querySelector('button, a') as HTMLElement | null)?.focus()`. Keep `aria-haspopup='true'` and `aria-expanded` toggling as-is. Verify the toolbar-handlers and render-browse tests still pass (they query buttons by text/className, not by role).

findings.title: Move/Copy prompts use developer jargon ('relative destination path')
findings.description: The Move and Copy actions use `window.prompt('Move to relative destination path:', entry.path)` and `window.prompt('Copy to relative destination path:', entry.path)`. 'Relative destination path' is internal/developer terminology that end users of a file browser will not understand; it also does not tell them the path is relative to the storage root or how separators work. The copy should be plain and action-oriented, consistent with the rest of the UI (e.g. 'New directory name:', 'Delete "x"?'). The prompts pre-fill `entry.path` as the default, which is a helpful hint and should stay.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/dom-builders.ts in `makeRowMenu`, change the Move handler's prompt string from `'Move to relative destination path:'` to `'Move "' + entry.name + '" to:'` and the Copy handler's prompt string from `'Copy to relative destination path:'` to `'Copy "' + entry.name + '" to:'`. Keep the second argument (`entry.path`) as the default value and the rest of the handler (null-check, `normalizeRelativePath`, API call, `rerender()`) unchanged. Update any toolbar-handlers/dom-builders test that asserts the literal old prompt string to expect the new one.

findings.title: Placeholder text and search icon sit just below the 4.5:1 AA contrast threshold
findings.description: The search input placeholder and the overlaid icons use `#9a9a9a` on the input background `#333337`. That combination measures ~4.47:1 contrast — fractionally under the 4.5:1 WCAG AA threshold for normal-size text (the placeholder is 13px, not large text). The same `#9a9a9a` on the body background `#1e1e1e` is fine (~5.9:1). Because the placeholder is the only label hint sighted users get before typing (and doubles as the search affordance), nudging it above threshold is worthwhile. Icon glyphs are held to the 3:1 non-text contrast threshold, which this passes, but raising the color also helps the clear/search icon legibility.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css, in the `.toolbar input[type='search']::placeholder, .toolbar input[type='text']::placeholder { color: #9a9a9a; }` rule, change the color to `#a8a8a8` (yields ~4.9:1 against `#333337`, clearing AA). Optionally bump `.search-wrapper .search-icon, .search-wrapper .clear-btn { color: ... }` from `#9a9a9a` to `#a8a8a8` for visual consistency. Do not change hover/active colors. If any CSS test asserts the literal `#9a9a9a` placeholder color, update it.

findings.title: Clear button has a hover state but no focus style and is below the 24px touch-target minimum
findings.description: The `.clear-btn` defines `.clear-btn:hover { color: #e6e6e6; }` but no `:focus`/`:focus-visible` rule, so unlike the toolbar input (which gains a blue focus border) and the upload label (which has an explicit `:focus-within` outline), keyboard focus on the clear button relies on the browser default outline only — inconsistent with the rest of the toolbar. The button is also only ~20px tall/wide (`bi-x-lg` ~16px glyph + `padding: 2px`), below the 24×24 CSS px minimum in WCAG 2.5.8 (Target Size, Minimum) and well under the 44px enhanced target. Add a visible focus ring matching the app accent and enlarge the hit area.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css in the `.search-wrapper .clear-btn` block, increase `padding` from `2px` to `6px` (and add `border-radius: 4px` is already inherited) so the hit area is at least 28×28px, and ensure the input's `padding-right` (currently `28px`) is increased to `32px` in `.search-wrapper input[type='text']` so typed text still does not slide under the larger button. Add a new rule `.search-wrapper .clear-btn:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid #6aa6ff; outline-offset: 1px; }`. Leave the `:hover` color rule as-is.

findings.title: Loading spinner shows for search but not for folder navigation, creating inconsistent feedback
findings.description: The new spinner is gated behind `if (route.view === 'search')`, so navigating into a folder (a `browse` route) clears the results and fetches with no loading affordance, while searching shows a spinner for the same kind of async fetch. On slow connections this looks like a hang or a frozen listing for browse navigation. The `render()` already clears `resultsEl.innerHTML` synchronously for both routes, so the browse path shows a blank panel until the fetch resolves. For consistent perceived performance, show the same spinner (or a neutral one) for browse routes too.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/render-orchestrator.ts, move the spinner construction out of the `if (route.view === 'search')` branch so it is appended for both `browse` and `search` routes. Concretely: remove the `if (route.view === 'search') {` guard around the `spinnerWrap`/`spinnerIcon` creation and `resultsEl.appendChild(spinnerWrap)` so the spinner shows whenever `render()` begins an async fetch; keep the rest (try/catch, renderBrowse/renderSearch, race-token checks) unchanged. The spinner is still replaced by the rendered table or error text because both `renderBrowse`/`renderSearch` and the catch block replace `resultsEl`'s contents. Adjust any render-orchestration test that asserted a spinner only appears for the search route.

The central fix is in `makeIcon` (covers all menu-item icons); the few icons created inline in app.ts and render-orchestrator.ts must be patched separately.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/dom-builders.ts, in the `makeIcon(name)` function, before returning the element add `icon.setAttribute('aria-hidden', 'true');`. Then in src/app.ts set the same attribute on `searchIcon` (the `bi bi-search search-icon` element) and on `clearIcon` (the `bi bi-x-lg` inside the clear button) right after each is created; also set it on `closeIcon` (the dialog close button's `bi bi-x-lg`). In src/app/render-orchestrator.ts set `aria-hidden='true'` on the spinner icon (`spinnerIcon`, class `bi bi-arrow-repeat spinning`). Run the existing dom-builders / start-app / render-orchestration tests; none should assert the absence of aria-hidden, but if a snapshot-like assertion fails, update it.

findings.title: Search loading spinner is visual-only; screen readers get no 'loading' announcement
findings.description: When a search is in flight, sighted users see a centered spinning `bi-arrow-repeat`, but the spinner container is a plain `<div>` with no `role='status'` and no `aria-live` region, so assistive-tech users receive no feedback that work is happening (and the previous results simply vanish with no explanation). WCAG 4.1.3 (Status Messages) calls for status messages to be announced programmatically. Mark the spinner wrapper as a polite live region so its presence is announced when it appears.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/render-orchestrator.ts, where the spinner is constructed (the `if (route.view === 'search')` block that builds `spinnerWrap` and `spinnerIcon`), add `spinnerWrap.setAttribute('role', 'status');` and `spinnerWrap.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Loading search results');` after creating `spinnerWrap`. Keep the spinner icon itself hidden from AT per the decorative-icons finding (it already has no text content). Do not change the visual styling.

findings.title: Row menu advertises `aria-haspopup='true'` (menu) but does not implement the menu pattern
findings.description: The per-row ⋮ button sets `aria-haspopup='true'` (which per spec is equivalent to `aria-haspopup='menu'`) and toggles `aria-expanded`, signaling to AT users that activating it opens a menu widget with arrow-key item navigation, Home/End, and focus moving to the first item on open. The actual dropdown is a plain `<div class='row-menu'>` containing `<button class='btn'>` / `<a class='btn'>` items with no `role='menu'`/`role='menuitem'`, no focus move into the menu on open, and no arrow-key handling — only Tab/Shift+Tab and Escape work. This expectation mismatch is confusing for screen-reader users. Either complete the menu semantics or downgrade to a plain disclosure. The lower-risk, self-contained fix is to complete the semantics by adding the roles.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/dom-builders.ts `createMenu` (which builds the shared `.row-menu` element), set `menu.setAttribute('role', 'menu')` on the created `menu` div, and set `role='menuitem'` on every element in the passed-in `items` array (iterate and set the attribute, skipping any element that already has a role). Then in `makeRowMenu`'s `openMenu`/click handler, after the menu is shown, focus the first item: add `(items[0] as HTMLElement)?.focus()` is not available here, so instead in `createMenu`'s `openMenu`, after positioning, call `(menu.querySelector('button, a') as HTMLElement | null)?.focus()`. Keep `aria-haspopup='true'` and `aria-expanded` toggling as-is. Verify the toolbar-handlers and render-browse tests still pass (they query buttons by text/className, not by role).

findings.title: Move/Copy prompts use developer jargon ('relative destination path')
findings.description: The Move and Copy actions use `window.prompt('Move to relative destination path:', entry.path)` and `window.prompt('Copy to relative destination path:', entry.path)`. 'Relative destination path' is internal/developer terminology that end users of a file browser will not understand; it also does not tell them the path is relative to the storage root or how separators work. The copy should be plain and action-oriented, consistent with the rest of the UI (e.g. 'New directory name:', 'Delete "x"?'). The prompts pre-fill `entry.path` as the default, which is a helpful hint and should stay.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/dom-builders.ts in `makeRowMenu`, change the Move handler's prompt string from `'Move to relative destination path:'` to `'Move "' + entry.name + '" to:'` and the Copy handler's prompt string from `'Copy to relative destination path:'` to `'Copy "' + entry.name + '" to:'`. Keep the second argument (`entry.path`) as the default value and the rest of the handler (null-check, `normalizeRelativePath`, API call, `rerender()`) unchanged. Update any toolbar-handlers/dom-builders test that asserts the literal old prompt string to expect the new one.

findings.title: Placeholder text and search icon sit just below the 4.5:1 AA contrast threshold
findings.description: The search input placeholder and the overlaid icons use `#9a9a9a` on the input background `#333337`. That combination measures ~4.47:1 contrast — fractionally under the 4.5:1 WCAG AA threshold for normal-size text (the placeholder is 13px, not large text). The same `#9a9a9a` on the body background `#1e1e1e` is fine (~5.9:1). Because the placeholder is the only label hint sighted users get before typing (and doubles as the search affordance), nudging it above threshold is worthwhile. Icon glyphs are held to the 3:1 non-text contrast threshold, which this passes, but raising the color also helps the clear/search icon legibility.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css, in the `.toolbar input[type='search']::placeholder, .toolbar input[type='text']::placeholder { color: #9a9a9a; }` rule, change the color to `#a8a8a8` (yields ~4.9:1 against `#333337`, clearing AA). Optionally bump `.search-wrapper .search-icon, .search-wrapper .clear-btn { color: ... }` from `#9a9a9a` to `#a8a8a8` for visual consistency. Do not change hover/active colors. If any CSS test asserts the literal `#9a9a9a` placeholder color, update it.

findings.title: Clear button has a hover state but no focus style and is below the 24px touch-target minimum
findings.description: The `.clear-btn` defines `.clear-btn:hover { color: #e6e6e6; }` but no `:focus`/`:focus-visible` rule, so unlike the toolbar input (which gains a blue focus border) and the upload label (which has an explicit `:focus-within` outline), keyboard focus on the clear button relies on the browser default outline only — inconsistent with the rest of the toolbar. The button is also only ~20px tall/wide (`bi-x-lg` ~16px glyph + `padding: 2px`), below the 24×24 CSS px minimum in WCAG 2.5.8 (Target Size, Minimum) and well under the 44px enhanced target. Add a visible focus ring matching the app accent and enlarge the hit area.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css in the `.search-wrapper .clear-btn` block, increase `padding` from `2px` to `6px` (and add `border-radius: 4px` is already inherited) so the hit area is at least 28×28px, and ensure the input's `padding-right` (currently `28px`) is increased to `32px` in `.search-wrapper input[type='text']` so typed text still does not slide under the larger button. Add a new rule `.search-wrapper .clear-btn:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid #6aa6ff; outline-offset: 1px; }`. Leave the `:hover` color rule as-is.

findings.title: Loading spinner shows for search but not for folder navigation, creating inconsistent feedback
findings.description: The new spinner is gated behind `if (route.view === 'search')`, so navigating into a folder (a `browse` route) clears the results and fetches with no loading affordance, while searching shows a spinner for the same kind of async fetch. On slow connections this looks like a hang or a frozen listing for browse navigation. The `render()` already clears `resultsEl.innerHTML` synchronously for both routes, so the browse path shows a blank panel until the fetch resolves. For consistent perceived performance, show the same spinner (or a neutral one) for browse routes too.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/render-orchestrator.ts, move the spinner construction out of the `if (route.view === 'search')` branch so it is appended for both `browse` and `search` routes. Concretely: remove the `if (route.view === 'search') {` guard around the `spinnerWrap`/`spinnerIcon` creation and `resultsEl.appendChild(spinnerWrap)` so the spinner shows whenever `render()` begins an async fetch; keep the rest (try/catch, renderBrowse/renderSearch, race-token checks) unchanged. The spinner is still replaced by the rendered table or error text because both `renderBrowse`/`renderSearch` and the catch block replace `resultsEl`'s contents. Adjust any render-orchestration test that asserted a spinner only appears for the search route.

output.summary: Reviewed the entire changeset (src/app.css, src/app.ts, src/app/dom-builders.ts, render-browse.ts, render-orchestrator.ts, toolbar-handlers.ts). The work is overwhelmingly UI-facing: a new search-wrapper with overlaid icon + CSS-driven clear button, debounced instant search, a loading spinner, Bootstrap Icons on every menu item, a right-click current-directory context menu, and whole-row click-to-browse. Visual styling and interaction polish are generally solid (correct box-sizing fix on menu anchors, pointer cursors on navigable rows, clamped menu positioning, focus-within reflection on the upload label). The main gaps are accessibility: the search input has no real accessible name (placeholder only), decorative icons are not hidden from AT, the spinner is not announced, the row-menu advertises menu semantics it does not implement, and a couple of small contrast/touch-target/copy issues.

findings.title: Search input is labeled only by its placeholder, with no accessible name
findings.description: The toolbar search input is created with `type='text'` and `placeholder='Search...'` but no `aria-label` and no associated `<label>`. A placeholder is not an accessible name: it vanishes the moment the user types, has low contrast, and is an unreliable/last-resort source in the accessible-name computation. Notably the upload input right next to it correctly gets `uploadInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Upload files')`, so this is an inconsistency. Screen-reader users get either no name or a fragile one. WCAG 1.3.1 (Info and Relationships) and 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value) require a programmatically determinable name. Add an `aria-label` (the wrapper holds no visible text label, so `aria-label` is the right mechanism, matching the upload input).
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.ts, in startApp where the search input is built (around the lines `const searchInput = document.createElement('input'); searchInput.type = 'text'; searchInput.placeholder = 'Search...';`), add `searchInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Search files');` immediately after the placeholder is set. Do not remove the placeholder (it doubles as the empty-state hint for sighted users and drives the `:placeholder-shown` clear-button toggle). Verify no existing test asserts the absence of an aria-label; if any does, update it to expect 'Search files'.

findings.description: The search input placeholder and the overlaid icons use `#9a9a9a` on the input background `#333337`. That combination measures ~4.47:1 contrast — fractionally under the 4.5:1 WCAG AA threshold for normal-size text (the placeholder is 13px, not large text). The same `#9a9a9a` on the body background `#1e1e1e` is fine (~5.9:1). Because the placeholder is the only label hint sighted users get before typing (and doubles as the search affordance), nudging it above threshold is worthwhile. Icon glyphs are held to the 3:1 non-text contrast threshold, which this passes, but raising the color also helps the clear/search icon legibility.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css, in the `.toolbar input[type='search']::placeholder, .toolbar input[type='text']::placeholder { color: #9a9a9a; }` rule, change the color to `#a8a8a8` (yields ~4.9:1 against `#333337`, clearing AA). Optionally bump `.search-wrapper .search-icon, .search-wrapper .clear-btn { color: ... }` from `#9a9a9a` to `#a8a8a8` for visual consistency. Do not change hover/active colors. If any CSS test asserts the literal `#9a9a9a` placeholder color, update it.

findings.title: Clear button has a hover state but no focus style and is below the 24px touch-target minimum
findings.description: The `.clear-btn` defines `.clear-btn:hover { color: #e6e6e6; }` but no `:focus`/`:focus-visible` rule, so unlike the toolbar input (which gains a blue focus border) and the upload label (which has an explicit `:focus-within` outline), keyboard focus on the clear button relies on the browser default outline only — inconsistent with the rest of the toolbar. The button is also only ~20px tall/wide (`bi-x-lg` ~16px glyph + `padding: 2px`), below the 24×24 CSS px minimum in WCAG 2.5.8 (Target Size, Minimum) and well under the 44px enhanced target. Add a visible focus ring matching the app accent and enlarge the hit area.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css in the `.search-wrapper .clear-btn` block, increase `padding` from `2px` to `6px` (and add `border-radius: 4px` is already inherited) so the hit area is at least 28×28px, and ensure the input's `padding-right` (currently `28px`) is increased to `32px` in `.search-wrapper input[type='text']` so typed text still does not slide under the larger button. Add a new rule `.search-wrapper .clear-btn:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid #6aa6ff; outline-offset: 1px; }`. Leave the `:hover` color rule as-is.

findings.title: Loading spinner shows for search but not for folder navigation, creating inconsistent feedback
findings.description: The new spinner is gated behind `if (route.view === 'search')`, so navigating into a folder (a `browse` route) clears the results and fetches with no loading affordance, while searching shows a spinner for the same kind of async fetch. On slow connections this looks like a hang or a frozen listing for browse navigation. The `render()` already clears `resultsEl.innerHTML` synchronously for both routes, so the browse path shows a blank panel until the fetch resolves. For consistent perceived performance, show the same spinner (or a neutral one) for browse routes too.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/render-orchestrator.ts, move the spinner construction out of the `if (route.view === 'search')` branch so it is appended for both `browse` and `search` routes. Concretely: remove the `if (route.view === 'search') {` guard around the `spinnerWrap`/`spinnerIcon` creation and `resultsEl.appendChild(spinnerWrap)` so the spinner shows whenever `render()` begins an async fetch; keep the rest (try/catch, renderBrowse/renderSearch, race-token checks) unchanged. The spinner is still replaced by the rendered table or error text because both `renderBrowse`/`renderSearch` and the catch block replace `resultsEl`'s contents. Adjust any render-orchestration test that asserted a spinner only appears for the search route.

The existing test suite for the search loading spinner covers the visual presence and lifecycle of the spinner but does not verify the accessibility attributes required by the fix (role='status' and aria-label='Loading search results' on the spinner wrapper). The current tests are well-structured and follow project patterns, but they lack coverage for the new WCAG 4.1.3 status message requirements. The fix instructions specify adding these attributes to the spinner wrapper, yet no test asserts their presence. Additionally, the existing test for aria-hidden='true' on the icon (lines 610-630) is a separate accessibility concern and does not cover the requested live region.

output.feedback: The fix is correctly and completely applied. In src/app.ts, `searchInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Search files');` was added immediately after `searchInput.placeholder = 'Search...';` (around line 77), exactly per the fix instructions. The placeholder is preserved as required (it doubles as the empty-state hint and drives the `:placeholder-shown` clear-button toggle). The aria-label value 'Search files' matches the instruction verbatim. The change is consistent with the existing project pattern: the sibling upload input uses `uploadInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Upload files')`, and the close/clear icon buttons use the same `setAttribute('aria-label', ...)` mechanism. A concise, accurate comment explains the WCAG 1.3.1/4.1.2 rationale and notes it matches the upload input. No test asserts the absence of an aria-label on the search input (start-app.test.ts lines 100-107 only assert type, placeholder, and structure), so no test updates were needed. No dead code, no scope expansion, no logic errors.

The core deliverable of this fix — appending step 8 to dev-diary.md — was not applied. The file still ends at step 7. The only test change is a new test in render-orchestration.test.ts that expects aria-hidden="true" on the spinner icon, but the production code never sets this attribute, so the test fails. A documentation-only fix should not introduce failing tests that require production code changes. If the intent is to also add WCAG 1.3.1 compliance for the spinner icon, that requires a separate production-code change (setting spinnerIcon.setAttribute('aria-hidden', 'true') in render-orchestrator.ts) before this test can pass.

issues.description: Step 8 documenting the instant-search-with-icons-and-spinner work was never appended. The file ends at step 7. This is the core deliverable of the documentation fix and it is missing entirely.

The new test 'hides the spinner icon from assistive tech via aria-hidden="true" (WCAG 1.3.1)' expects spinningIcon.getAttribute('aria-hidden') to be 'true', but the production code (render-orchestrator.ts:82) never sets aria-hidden on the spinner icon. The test fails with: expected null to be 'true'. A documentation fix should not introduce failing tests. Either the test should be removed or the production code should be updated to set aria-hidden first.

output.summary: Reviewed all 14 committed files in the 'instant search with icons and spinner' workflow (debounced search, search-wrapper DOM restructure, clear button, CSS spinner, ESLint configs, and extensive tests). The production code (app.ts, toolbar-handlers.ts, render-orchestrator.ts, app.css) is generally well-structured with thorough documentation. However, I found a committed .bak artifact, a dead/duplicate ESLint config that imports a non-existent dependency, acknowledged DRY violations in the CSS test suite (two identical describe blocks), significant behavioral test duplication across three test files, and a misleading no-op event listener in a test. No critical correctness bugs were found in the production logic.

findings.title: Committed .bak backup file is a leftover artifact
findings.description: The file `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak` is a verbatim copy of the OLD version of `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts` (before the `clearSearch` function was added). It was committed to the repository (confirmed via `git show HEAD:src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak`). It is dead code: no build tool or import references it, `.bak` is not in `.gitignore`, and it will confuse future readers who may wonder which file is authoritative. While `tsc` currently ignores it (it only matches `src/**/*.ts`), it pollutes the source tree and violates the project's clean-structure convention. It should be deleted entirely.
findings.fixPrompt: Delete the file `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak` from the repository. It is a stale backup of `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts` from before the `clearSearch` function was added, committed by accident. Run `git rm src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak` and commit the deletion. No code references this file.

findings.title: Duplicate ESLint config imports non-existent dependency and is shadowed by eslint.config.mjs
findings.description: Two ESLint flat configs now coexist: `eslint.config.js` (added in task t-06) and `eslint.config.mjs` (added in task t-07). ESLint's flat config resolution order loads `.mjs` before `.js`, so `eslint.config.js` is NEVER loaded — it is dead code. Worse, `eslint.config.js` imports `@typescript-eslint/parser`, which is NOT listed in `package.json` dependencies and is not in `node_modules`. The `.mjs` file explicitly documents this: 'ESLint cannot parse TypeScript syntax without the typescript-eslint parser (not installed here), so a lenient passthrough parser is used.' If someone deletes `eslint.config.mjs` (or the resolution order changes), ESLint would crash trying to resolve the missing import. The `.js` file should be deleted, leaving only the well-documented `.mjs` config.
findings.fixPrompt: Delete the file `eslint.config.js`. It is a dead duplicate of `eslint.config.mjs`: ESLint loads `.mjs` first (higher priority in flat config resolution), so `.js` is never used. Additionally, `eslint.config.js` imports `@typescript-eslint/parser` which is not a project dependency (not in package.json), so it would crash if ever loaded. The surviving `eslint.config.mjs` already handles the same purpose correctly with a lenient passthrough parser and full documentation. Run `git rm eslint.config.js` and commit.

findings.title: Two identical describe('spinner animation') blocks and two overlapping search-wrapper test blocks test the same CSS
findings.description: The file `src/app.css.test.ts` contains two `describe('src/app.css — spinner animation', ...)` blocks (one starting around line 547 using `parseRules`-based helpers, another around line 685 using `ruleDeclarations`). There are also two overlapping search-wrapper test sections: `describe('src/app.css — search input wrapper (.search-wrapper)', ...)` (line ~424) and `describe('src/app.css — search wrapper and in-input icons', ...)` (line ~623). The second block's own header comment explicitly acknowledges the duplication: 'A focused restatement of the search-wrapper / icon / spinner contract that uses the simple first-match ruleDeclarations helper (instead of the parseRules-based helpers used in the blocks above).' This doubles the maintenance burden: any CSS change to `.search-wrapper`, `.clear-btn`, `.spinning`, or `.search-spinner` must be updated in two places. One of each pair should be removed.
findings.fixPrompt: In `src/app.css.test.ts`, remove the DUPLICATE test blocks that re-test the same CSS rules already covered by earlier blocks. Specifically: (1) Remove the second `describe('src/app.css — spinner animation', ...)` block (the one near the end of the file that uses `ruleDeclarations` and tests `@keyframes spin`, `.spinning`, and `.search-spinner` — these are already tested by the earlier `parseRules`-based spinner block). (2) Remove the `describe('src/app.css — search wrapper and in-input icons', ...)` block (the one whose comment says 'A focused restatement of the search-wrapper / icon / spinner contract') — its assertions about `.search-wrapper` position/display/margin, `.clear-btn` display:none, and `:placeholder-shown` toggling are already covered by the earlier `parseRules`-based blocks. Keep the earlier, more comprehensive `parseRules`-based blocks. Run `npx vitest run src/app.css.test.ts` to confirm all remaining tests still pass.

findings.title: Search event behaviors (debounce, Enter, Escape, clear) tested redundantly in both search-wrapper.test.ts and toolbar-handlers.test.ts
findings.description: The debounce, Enter-key, Escape-key, and clear-button-click behaviors are tested in BOTH `src/app/search-wrapper.test.ts` (describe 'startApp search area events') AND `src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts` (describe 'search' and 'clearSearch'). For example: 'resets the debounce window on each keystroke' (search-wrapper.test.ts) vs 'resets the debounce timer on each keystroke' (toolbar-handlers.test.ts); 'triggers search immediately on Enter' vs 'still navigates immediately when Enter is pressed'; 'stops propagation on Escape' vs 'Escape key stops propagation'; 'clears the search input value when the clear button is clicked' vs 'clears the search input and navigates to browse when the X (clear) button is clicked'. The DOM structure tests also overlap between `search-wrapper.test.ts` and `start-app.test.ts`. This triple-testing of the same behaviors means any behavioral change requires updating tests in 2-3 files. The `search-wrapper.test.ts` file (404 lines, entirely new) is the most expansive and overlaps most; its behavioral event tests should be consolidated into `toolbar-handlers.test.ts` (which tests the handlers by name) and only the DOM-structure/DomRefs assertions unique to `search-wrapper.test.ts` should remain.
findings.fixPrompt: Consolidate the duplicated search-event behavioral tests. In `src/app/search-wrapper.test.ts`, remove the `describe('startApp search area events', ...)` block (lines ~197-383) which tests debounce, Enter, Escape, and clear-button click behaviors — these are ALL already covered by `src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts`'s `describe('search', ...)` and `describe('clearSearch', ...)` blocks. Keep only the `describe('startApp search area structure', ...)` block (DOM structure assertions) and the `describe('startApp search area: DomRefs unchanged', ...)` block in `search-wrapper.test.ts`, as those are unique. Also remove the overlapping 'does NOT render a Search button' test from `start-app.test.ts` since `search-wrapper.test.ts` already covers it. Run `npx vitest run` to confirm all behaviors remain tested after consolidation.

findings.title: Escape stopPropagation test adds a no-op event listener with misleading comment
findings.description: The test 'Escape key stops propagation (does not close the parent <dialog>)' adds a `keydown` listener on `parentEl` with an empty callback body: `parentEl.addEventListener('keydown', () => { // should NOT fire if stopPropagation worked });`. This listener never asserts anything — its body is empty. The comment implies the listener is used to verify non-propagation, but the actual assertion is solely `expect(stopSpy).toHaveBeenCalled()`. The listener is dead/misleading code that suggests a verification the test does not actually perform. Either remove the listener entirely (the stopSpy assertion is sufficient) or make it actually assert (e.g., track whether it fired and assert it did not).
findings.fixPrompt: In `src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts`, find the test named 'Escape key stops propagation (does not close the parent <dialog>)'. Remove the no-op `parentEl.addEventListener('keydown', () => { // should NOT fire if stopPropagation worked });` lines — the listener has an empty body and never asserts anything, making it dead code with a misleading comment. The test's real assertion (`expect(stopSpy).toHaveBeenCalled()`) already verifies the contract. After removing the listener, confirm the test still passes with `npx vitest run src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts`.

output.summary: Reviewed all 14 committed files in the 'instant search with icons and spinner' workflow (debounced search, search-wrapper DOM restructure, clear button, CSS spinner, ESLint configs, and extensive tests). The production code (app.ts, toolbar-handlers.ts, render-orchestrator.ts, app.css) is generally well-structured with thorough documentation. However, I found a committed .bak artifact, a dead/duplicate ESLint config that imports a non-existent dependency, acknowledged DRY violations in the CSS test suite (two identical describe blocks), significant behavioral test duplication across three test files, and a misleading no-op event listener in a test. No critical correctness bugs were found in the production logic.

findings.title: Committed .bak backup file is a leftover artifact
findings.description: The file `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak` is a verbatim copy of the OLD version of `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts` (before the `clearSearch` function was added). It was committed to the repository (confirmed via `git show HEAD:src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak`). It is dead code: no build tool or import references it, `.bak` is not in `.gitignore`, and it will confuse future readers who may wonder which file is authoritative. While `tsc` currently ignores it (it only matches `src/**/*.ts`), it pollutes the source tree and violates the project's clean-structure convention. It should be deleted entirely.
findings.fixPrompt: Delete the file `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak` from the repository. It is a stale backup of `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts` from before the `clearSearch` function was added, committed by accident. Run `git rm src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak` and commit the deletion. No code references this file.

findings.title: Duplicate ESLint config imports non-existent dependency and is shadowed by eslint.config.mjs
findings.description: Two ESLint flat configs now coexist: `eslint.config.js` (added in task t-06) and `eslint.config.mjs` (added in task t-07). ESLint's flat config resolution order loads `.mjs` before `.js`, so `eslint.config.js` is NEVER loaded — it is dead code. Worse, `eslint.config.js` imports `@typescript-eslint/parser`, which is NOT listed in `package.json` dependencies and is not in `node_modules`. The `.mjs` file explicitly documents this: 'ESLint cannot parse TypeScript syntax without the typescript-eslint parser (not installed here), so a lenient passthrough parser is used.' If someone deletes `eslint.config.mjs` (or the resolution order changes), ESLint would crash trying to resolve the missing import. The `.js` file should be deleted, leaving only the well-documented `.mjs` config.
findings.fixPrompt: Delete the file `eslint.config.js`. It is a dead duplicate of `eslint.config.mjs`: ESLint loads `.mjs` first (higher priority in flat config resolution), so `.js` is never used. Additionally, `eslint.config.js` imports `@typescript-eslint/parser` which is not a project dependency (not in package.json), so it would crash if ever loaded. The surviving `eslint.config.mjs` already handles the same purpose correctly with a lenient passthrough parser and full documentation. Run `git rm eslint.config.js` and commit.

Also remove the overlapping 'does NOT render a Search button' test from `start-app.test.ts` since `search-wrapper.test.ts` already covers it. Run `npx vitest run` to confirm all behaviors remain tested after consolidation.

findings.title: Escape stopPropagation test adds a no-op event listener with misleading comment
findings.description: The test 'Escape key stops propagation (does not close the parent <dialog>)' adds a `keydown` listener on `parentEl` with an empty callback body: `parentEl.addEventListener('keydown', () => { // should NOT fire if stopPropagation worked });`. This listener never asserts anything — its body is empty. The comment implies the listener is used to verify non-propagation, but the actual assertion is solely `expect(stopSpy).toHaveBeenCalled()`. The listener is dead/misleading code that suggests a verification the test does not actually perform. Either remove the listener entirely (the stopSpy assertion is sufficient) or make it actually assert (e.g., track whether it fired and assert it did not).
findings.fixPrompt: In `src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts`, find the test named 'Escape key stops propagation (does not close the parent <dialog>)'. Remove the no-op `parentEl.addEventListener('keydown', () => { // should NOT fire if stopPropagation worked });` lines — the listener has an empty body and never asserts anything, making it dead code with a misleading comment. The test's real assertion (`expect(stopSpy).toHaveBeenCalled()`) already verifies the contract. After removing the listener, confirm the test still passes with `npx vitest run src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts`.

output.summary: Reviewed all 14 committed files in the 'instant search with icons and spinner' workflow (debounced search, search-wrapper DOM restructure, clear button, CSS spinner, ESLint configs, and extensive tests). The production code (app.ts, toolbar-handlers.ts, render-orchestrator.ts, app.css) is generally well-structured with thorough documentation. However, I found a committed .bak artifact, a dead/duplicate ESLint config that imports a non-existent dependency, acknowledged DRY violations in the CSS test suite (two identical describe blocks), significant behavioral test duplication across three test files, and a misleading no-op event listener in a test. No critical correctness bugs were found in the production logic.

findings.title: Committed .bak backup file is a leftover artifact
findings.description: The file `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak` is a verbatim copy of the OLD version of `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts` (before the `clearSearch` function was added). It was committed to the repository (confirmed via `git show HEAD:src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak`). It is dead code: no build tool or import references it, `.bak` is not in `.gitignore`, and it will confuse future readers who may wonder which file is authoritative. While `tsc` currently ignores it (it only matches `src/**/*.ts`), it pollutes the source tree and violates the project's clean-structure convention. It should be deleted entirely.
findings.fixPrompt: Delete the file `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak` from the repository. It is a stale backup of `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts` from before the `clearSearch` function was added, committed by accident. Run `git rm src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak` and commit the deletion. No code references this file.

findings.title: Duplicate ESLint config imports non-existent dependency and is shadowed by eslint.config.mjs
findings.description: Two ESLint flat configs now coexist: `eslint.config.js` (added in task t-06) and `eslint.config.mjs` (added in task t-07). ESLint's flat config resolution order loads `.mjs` before `.js`, so `eslint.config.js` is NEVER loaded — it is dead code. Worse, `eslint.config.js` imports `@typescript-eslint/parser`, which is NOT listed in `package.json` dependencies and is not in `node_modules`. The `.mjs` file explicitly documents this: 'ESLint cannot parse TypeScript syntax without the typescript-eslint parser (not installed here), so a lenient passthrough parser is used.' If someone deletes `eslint.config.mjs` (or the resolution order changes), ESLint would crash trying to resolve the missing import. The `.js` file should be deleted, leaving only the well-documented `.mjs` config.
findings.fixPrompt: Delete the file `eslint.config.js`. It is a dead duplicate of `eslint.config.mjs`: ESLint loads `.mjs` first (higher priority in flat config resolution), so `.js` is never used. Additionally, `eslint.config.js` imports `@typescript-eslint/parser` which is not a project dependency (not in package.json), so it would crash if ever loaded. The surviving `eslint.config.mjs` already handles the same purpose correctly with a lenient passthrough parser and full documentation. Run `git rm eslint.config.js` and commit.

findings.title: Two identical describe('spinner animation') blocks and two overlapping search-wrapper test blocks test the same CSS
findings.description: The file `src/app.css.test.ts` contains two `describe('src/app.css — spinner animation', ...)` blocks (one starting around line 547 using `parseRules`-based helpers, another around line 685 using `ruleDeclarations`). There are also two overlapping search-wrapper test sections: `describe('src/app.css — search input wrapper (.search-wrapper)', ...)` (line ~424) and `describe('src/app.css — search wrapper and in-input icons', ...)` (line ~623). The second block's own header comment explicitly acknowledges the duplication: 'A focused restatement of the search-wrapper / icon / spinner contract that uses the simple first-match ruleDeclarations helper (instead of the parseRules-based helpers used in the blocks above).' This doubles the maintenance burden: any CSS change to `.search-wrapper`, `.clear-btn`, `.spinning`, or `.search-spinner` must be updated in two places. One of each pair should be removed.
findings.fixPrompt: In `src/app.css.test.ts`, remove the DUPLICATE test blocks that re-test the same CSS rules already covered by earlier blocks. Specifically: (1) Remove the second `describe('src/app.css — spinner animation', ...)` block (the one near the end of the file that uses `ruleDeclarations` and tests `@keyframes spin`, `.spinning`, and `.search-spinner` — these are already tested by the earlier `parseRules`-based spinner block). (2) Remove the `describe('src/app.css — search wrapper and in-input icons', ...)` block (the one whose comment says 'A focused restatement of the search-wrapper / icon / spinner contract') — its assertions about `.search-wrapper` position/display/margin, `.clear-btn` display:none, and `:placeholder-shown` toggling are already covered by the earlier `parseRules`-based blocks. Keep the earlier, more comprehensive `parseRules`-based blocks. Run `npx vitest run src/app.css.test.ts` to confirm all remaining tests still pass.

findings.title: Search event behaviors (debounce, Enter, Escape, clear) tested redundantly in both search-wrapper.test.ts and toolbar-handlers.test.ts
findings.description: The debounce, Enter-key, Escape-key, and clear-button-click behaviors are tested in BOTH `src/app/search-wrapper.test.ts` (describe 'startApp search area events') AND `src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts` (describe 'search' and 'clearSearch'). For example: 'resets the debounce window on each keystroke' (search-wrapper.test.ts) vs 'resets the debounce timer on each keystroke' (toolbar-handlers.test.ts); 'triggers search immediately on Enter' vs 'still navigates immediately when Enter is pressed'; 'stops propagation on Escape' vs 'Escape key stops propagation'; 'clears the search input value when the clear button is clicked' vs 'clears the search input and navigates to browse when the X (clear) button is clicked'. The DOM structure tests also overlap between `search-wrapper.test.ts` and `start-app.test.ts`. This triple-testing of the same behaviors means any behavioral change requires updating tests in 2-3 files. The `search-wrapper.test.ts` file (404 lines, entirely new) is the most expansive and overlaps most; its behavioral event tests should be consolidated into `toolbar-handlers.test.ts` (which tests the handlers by name) and only the DOM-structure/DomRefs assertions unique to `search-wrapper.test.ts` should remain.
findings.fixPrompt: Consolidate the duplicated search-event behavioral tests. In `src/app/search-wrapper.test.ts`, remove the `describe('startApp search area events', ...)` block (lines ~197-383) which tests debounce, Enter, Escape, and clear-button click behaviors — these are ALL already covered by `src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts`'s `describe('search', ...)` and `describe('clearSearch', ...)` blocks. Keep only the `describe('startApp search area structure', ...)` block (DOM structure assertions) and the `describe('startApp search area: DomRefs unchanged', ...)` block in `search-wrapper.test.ts`, as those are unique. Also remove the overlapping 'does NOT render a Search button' test from `start-app.test.ts` since `search-wrapper.test.ts` already covers it. Run `npx vitest run` to confirm all behaviors remain tested after consolidation.

The test file should include assertions for these attributes to ensure the accessibility fix is properly validated.

Note: there is one pre-existing test failure in 'becomes visible + interactive on row hover and when its menu is open' (ruleDeclarations for .row-menu-btn[aria-expanded="true"] returns null), which is unrelated to this finding.

The test suite already contains comprehensive tests that cover the fix requirements. All tests that need to pass after the fix are already written in render-browse.test.ts: (1) role='menu' on .row-menu (line 626-632), (2) role='menuitem' on every menu item (line 742-756), (3) focus moves to first menu item on open (line 353-364 for button click, line 392-403 for right-click), and (4) directory menu also carries proper roles (line 1061-1069). These tests are currently failing because they expect the fix behavior but the implementation hasn't been applied yet. No additional tests need to be written - the existing tests comprehensively validate the accessibility semantics and focus management described in the fix instructions. The test patterns follow existing conventions, are well-documented, and test the expected behavior from both the API perspective (role attributes) and user perspective (focus management).

The test changes submitted do not address the Move/Copy prompt jargon fix at all. All changes in the working tree (render-browse.test.ts, dom-builders.test.ts, render-orchestration.test.ts, start-app.test.ts) are accessibility improvements (WCAG 1.3.1 aria-hidden on decorative icons, WCAG 4.1.3 role='status' for spinner, role='menu'/role='menuitem' patterns, focus management). While these are valid improvements, they are unrelated to the prompt string fix. No tests anywhere in the suite assert the Move/Copy prompt string text — neither the old jargon strings ('Move to relative destination path:') nor the new user-friendly strings ('Move "x" to:'). The only prompt string assertion in the entire test suite is for 'New directory name:' at render-browse.test.ts:1098. The fix instructions require updating tests that assert the old prompt strings, but since none exist, the coverage gap needs to be filled with new tests.

issues.description: Missing tests: No tests assert the Move/Copy prompt string text. The existing tests for Move/Copy (lines 267-320) only verify API call parameters (sourcePath/destinationPath) and null-return behavior, but never assert the first argument of window.prompt (the message text shown to the user). Tests should be added that assert expect(window.prompt).toHaveBeenCalledWith('Move "a.txt" to:', 'docs/a.txt') and expect(window.prompt).toHaveBeenCalledWith('Copy "a.txt" to:', 'docs/a.txt') — analogous to the existing assertion at line 1098: expect(window.prompt).toHaveBeenCalledWith('New directory name:', '').

Production code unchanged: Lines 336 and 350 still use the old developer jargon strings 'Move to relative destination path:' and 'Copy to relative destination path:'. The fix requires changing these to 'Move "' + entry.name + "' to:'" and 'Copy "' + entry.name + "' to:'" respectively. This is a production code issue, not a test issue, but it means any prompt string tests would currently fail.

The existing Move/Copy tests at lines 267-320 (e.g. 'Move prompts for a destination then calls api.move with the normalized path') mock window.prompt but never assert the first argument (the prompt message). The prompt message is the user-facing text that this fix targets, so the test gap is directly in the area of the existing tests.

output.feedback:
The tests for the stated fix (WCAG 1.3.1 — hiding decorative Bootstrap-Icons from assistive tech via aria-hidden) are well-written, comprehensive, and correctly structured across four test files. The dom-builders.test.ts parameterized suite covers all 6 menu-item icon names plus arbitrary names; the start-app.test.ts suite covers every inline icon (searchIcon, clearIcon, closeIcon) plus a catch-all guard for all <i> elements; the render-orchestration.test.ts covers the spinner icon; and render-browse.test.ts adds inline aria-hidden assertions on menu-item and directory-menu icon glyphs. All tests follow existing patterns, are focused on one behavior each, and include clear WCAG-referencing descriptions.

However, this changeset also adds 8 tests/assertions that go BEYOND the stated fix scope and test functionality the fix instructions do not ask to implement. These will fail after the aria-hidden fix is applied, creating false negatives:
1. render-orchestration.test.ts: 3 failures testing WCAG 4.1.3 (spinner wrapper role="status" and aria-label) — the fix instructions only address aria-hidden on the spinner icon, not the wrapper's role/label.
2. render-browse.test.ts: 5 failures testing menu ARIA semantics (role="menu" on .row-menu, role="menuitem" on items, focus-into-menu behavior, directory menu role="menu"/"menuitem") — none of which are mentioned in the fix instructions.

These out-of-scope tests document legitimate accessibility gaps but should either be separated into a distinct changeset or the fix scope should be expanded to cover them.

issues.description: Three new tests/assertions test WCAG 4.1.3 (spinner wrapper role="status" and aria-label="Loading search results") which is beyond the stated fix scope of aria-hidden on decorative icons. The fix instructions only ask to set aria-hidden on spinnerIcon, not role/aria-label on spinnerWrap. These tests will fail after the aria-hidden fix is applied. Either remove/defer these tests or expand the fix scope to include role="status" and aria-label on the spinner wrapper.

Five new tests assert menu ARIA semantics (role="menu" on .row-menu, role="menuitem" on every menu item, focus-into-menu on open, directory menu role="menu"/role="menuitem") that require changes to createMenu() and focus management — none of which are in the fix instructions. These tests will fail after the aria-hidden-only fix is applied. Either remove/defer these tests or expand the fix scope to include menu widget semantics.
