Five new tests assert menu ARIA semantics (role="menu" on .row-menu, role="menuitem" on every menu item, focus-into-menu on open, directory menu role="menu"/role="menuitem") that require changes to createMenu() and focus management — none of which are in the fix instructions. These tests will fail after the aria-hidden-only fix is applied. Either remove/defer these tests or expand the fix scope to include menu widget semantics.

output.feedback:
The tests for the stated fix (WCAG 1.3.1 — hiding decorative Bootstrap-Icons from assistive tech via aria-hidden) are well-written, comprehensive, and correctly structured across four test files. The dom-builders.test.ts parameterized suite covers all 6 menu-item icon names plus arbitrary names; the start-app.test.ts suite covers every inline icon (searchIcon, clearIcon, closeIcon) plus a catch-all guard for all <i> elements; the render-orchestration.test.ts covers the spinner icon; and render-browse.test.ts adds inline aria-hidden assertions on menu-item and directory-menu icon glyphs. All tests follow existing patterns, are focused on one behavior each, and include clear WCAG-referencing descriptions.

However, this changeset also adds 8 tests/assertions that go BEYOND the stated fix scope and test functionality the fix instructions do not ask to implement. These will fail after the aria-hidden fix is applied, creating false negatives:
1. render-orchestration.test.ts: 3 failures testing WCAG 4.1.3 (spinner wrapper role="status" and aria-label) — the fix instructions only address aria-hidden on the spinner icon, not the wrapper's role/label.
2. render-browse.test.ts: 5 failures testing menu ARIA semantics (role="menu" on .row-menu, role="menuitem" on items, focus-into-menu behavior, directory menu role="menu"/"menuitem") — none of which are mentioned in the fix instructions.

These out-of-scope tests document legitimate accessibility gaps but should either be separated into a distinct changeset or the fix scope should be expanded to cover them.

output.feedback: The test file `src/eslint-config.test.ts` is well-written, comprehensive, and correctly implements regression tests for the ESLint config duplication fix. Key strengths: (1) Clear, descriptive test names that explain the expected behavior. (2) Comprehensive coverage spanning file existence, config count, parser dependency absence from package.json and node_modules, and the surviving config's loadability/self-containedness. (3) Follows the existing project test patterns — uses `// @vitest-environment node`, resolves paths via `import.meta.url`, and structures tests with `describe`/`it` blocks similar to `readme.test.ts` and `index.html.test.ts`. (4) Each test focuses on a single behavior and the tests are independent. (5) No over-mocking — filesystem reads are appropriate for these configuration-hygiene tests. (6) The dynamic import test (`exports a flat config array`) is particularly valuable as it proves the `.mjs` config actually loads without error. (7) Checking all dependency sections (`dependencies`, `devDependencies`, `peerDependencies`, `optionalDependencies`) in package.json is thorough. The tests will pass only after `eslint.config.js` is deleted and the commit is made, which is the correct expected behavior for regression tests written alongside a fix.

output.feedback: The fix correctly and minimally resolves the finding. Both duplicate describe blocks were removed exactly as specified: (1) the second `describe('src/app.css — spinner animation')` (the ruleDeclarations/CSS.match-based one re-testing @keyframes spin / .spinning / .search-spinner) and (2) the `describe('src/app.css — search wrapper and in-input icons')` block whose header comment explicitly called itself 'A focused restatement of the search-wrapper / icon / spinner contract'. The earlier, more comprehensive parseRules-based blocks for .search-wrapper, the wrapped input, overlay icons, :placeholder-shown toggling, and the spinner are all retained, so no CSS contract coverage was lost. All shared helpers (ruleDeclarations, stripComments, parseRules, RULES, rulesMatching, hasDecl, ruleWith) remain referenced by surviving blocks — no orphaned/dead code was introduced. The file terminates cleanly after the 'new rules placed in the toolbar section' block with no trailing blank lines or dangling comments. Test counts confirm the change: 50 tests (1 failing) before -> 41 tests (1 failing) after, i.e. exactly the 9 duplicate-block tests removed and nothing else affected. The single remaining test failure (row-menu-btn > becomes visible + interactive on row hover, asserting .row-menu-btn[aria-expanded="true"]) is PRE-EXISTING and unrelated to this dedup fix — verified via git stash that it fails identically without this change, and it lives in a describe block untouched by the diff (caused by separate CSS changes elsewhere in the working tree). The fix is correct and complete. The production code in dom-builders.ts has been updated to replace the developer jargon strings with plain, action-oriented prompts: 'Move to relative destination path:' → 'Move "' + entry.name + '" to:' and 'Copy to relative destination path:' → 'Copy "' + entry.name + '" to:'. The second argument (entry.path) remains as the pre-filled default value, and the rest of the handler logic (null-check, normalizeRelativePath, API call, rerender()) is unchanged. The existing tests in render-browse.test.ts already assert the new prompt strings (e.g. expect(window.prompt).toHaveBeenCalledWith('Move "a.txt" to:', 'docs/a.txt')) and now pass. The 2 remaining test failures (CSS opacity/pointer-events rule test, focus-management test) are pre-existing and unrelated to this fix.

The production code in src/app/render-orchestrator.ts is correct and complete — the spinner wrapper has role='status', aria-label='Loading search results', and the icon has aria-hidden='true', all with thorough documentation. However, the review feedback history (Attempts 1 and 2) specifically and repeatedly asked for test assertions verifying these accessibility attributes, and those assertions are still absent from src/app/render-orchestration.test.ts. The existing spinner test (lines 546-572) checks for the .search-spinner element and the .bi-arrow-repeat.spinning icon but does NOT assert role='status', aria-label='Loading search results', or aria-hidden='true' on the icon. This is the third review pass and the same gap persists. The natural place to add these is within the existing 'shows a spinner' test (or a dedicated accessibility test), asserting spinner.getAttribute('role') === 'status', spinner.getAttribute('aria-label') === 'Loading search results', and spinningIcon.getAttribute('aria-hidden') === 'true'.

issues.description: Missing test assertions for the WCAG 4.1.3 accessibility attributes on the search loading spinner. The review feedback has requested this twice (Attempts 1 and 2), but the spinner test block (lines 546-606) still only checks CSS class presence and icon class — it does not verify role='status', aria-label='Loading search results' on the .search-spinner wrapper, or aria-hidden='true' on the icon element. Add assertions in the existing 'shows a spinner while a search fetch is in flight' test (or a new focused test) to validate these attributes are set, ensuring the accessibility fix is properly covered.

The existing spinner test (lines 546-572) checks for the .search-spinner element and the .bi-arrow-repeat.spinning icon but does NOT assert role='status', aria-label='Loading search results', or aria-hidden='true' on the icon. This is the third review pass and the same gap persists. The natural place to add these is within the existing 'shows a spinner' test (or a dedicated accessibility test), asserting spinner.getAttribute('role') === 'status', spinner.getAttribute('aria-label') === 'Loading search results', and spinningIcon.getAttribute('aria-hidden') === 'true'.

output.feedback: The fix correctly resolves the finding. The stale backup file `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts.bak` has been deleted from the working tree (confirmed absent via `ls` and `git status` shows it as deleted). The authoritative `src/app/toolbar-handlers.ts` retains the `clearSearch` function (line 37), confirming the `.bak` was indeed the stale pre-`clearSearch` copy. A grep across the repo confirms no build tool, import, or config references the deleted file. Beyond the literal deletion, a regression guard `src/no-backup-files.test.ts` was added that (a) scans all of `src/` for any `.bak` file and (b) explicitly asserts the specific toolbar backup is absent. This follows the project's existing convention of meta/regression tests co-located in `src/` alongside `eslint-config.test.ts`, `readme.test.ts`, `index.html.test.ts`, and `app.css.test.ts`, uses the same `node` vitest environment, and passes (2 tests, 2 passed). The test is well-documented with a docstring explaining the original finding. No quality issues found. Note: the deletion and new test are currently unstaged/untracked in git (not yet committed), but that is a process step outside the code-review scope; the code changes themselves are correct and complete.

The fix is complete and the prior review feedback has been fully addressed. In src/app/render-orchestrator.ts, the spinner wrapper sets role='status' and aria-label='Loading search results' (establishing a polite live region per WCAG 4.1.3), and the decorative spinner icon is marked aria-hidden='true'. The implementation matches the fix instructions exactly and includes thorough documentation explaining the accessibility rationale. The previously missing test coverage requested in Attempts 1-3 is now present in src/app/render-orchestration.test.ts (lines ~558-574): the 'shows a spinner while a search fetch is in flight' test asserts spinner.getAttribute('role') === 'status', spinner.getAttribute('aria-label') === 'Loading search results', and spinningIcon.getAttribute('aria-hidden') === 'true', with explanatory comments tying each assertion to WCAG 4.1.3 / 1.3.1. All 20 tests in the file pass. The assertions are co-located with the existing spinner lifecycle test, follow the project's assertion-message pattern, and do not expand scope. No issues found.

The finding is fully resolved. Source strings changed exactly as specified: Move handler now uses 'Move "<name>" to:' and Copy handler uses 'Copy "<name>" to:', with entry.path retained as the pre-filled default and the rest of each handler (null-check, normalizeRelativePath, API call, rerender()) unchanged. The Attempt 1 coverage gap is filled: two new tests in src/app/render-browse.test.ts assert the exact new prompt strings via expect(window.prompt).toHaveBeenCalledWith('Move "a.txt" to:', 'docs/a.txt') (line 280) and expect(window.prompt).toHaveBeenCalledWith('Copy "a.txt" to:', 'docs/a.txt') (line 319), each with a comment documenting that the copy must be plain and action-oriented rather than internal jargon. Both targeted tests pass. No scope creep — changes are limited to the two prompt strings and their two new tests.

The aria-hidden fix is correctly and completely applied at all 5 icon creation sites specified in the fix instructions: makeIcon (dom-builders.ts:563), searchIcon (app.ts:87), clearIcon (app.ts:94), closeIcon (app.ts:155), and spinnerIcon (render-orchestrator.ts:91). Each uses the consistent setAttribute('aria-hidden', 'true') pattern matching existing conventions. The makeIcon doc comment was thoughtfully updated to explain the rationale (decorative glyph paired with visible text label). Dedicated tests verify the fix in dom-builders.test.ts (makeIcon + prepended icon), start-app.test.ts (search/clear/close icons), and render-orchestration.test.ts (spinner icon). All aria-hidden-specific tests pass. NOTE: There is one failing test in render-browse.test.ts:872 ('opening the menu moves focus onto the first menu item') but it is unrelated to this fix — it tests menu focus management from a separate createMenu change. The aria-hidden attribute does not affect element ordering or tag names, so this failure is out of scope and should not block this task.

output.feedback:
The menu-pattern fix is implemented correctly in spirit: `role="menu"` is set on the `.row-menu` div, `role="menuitem"` is set on every passed item (skipping items that already carry a role), `aria-haspopup`/`aria-expanded` are left intact, and focus is moved into the menu on open. The dom-builders and toolbar-handlers test suites pass.

However, the change introduces a FAILING test in render-browse.test.ts, violating the task's explicit requirement that 'the toolbar-handlers and render-browse tests still pass.' Root cause: both the production code (`createMenu`'s `openMenu`) and the newly-added test use `menu.querySelector('button, a')` to find the first focusable item. I confirmed via direct experimentation that happy-dom evaluates a comma-separated selector list in SELECTOR-LIST ORDER rather than the spec-mandated DOCUMENT ORDER: on a menu whose children are `[<a Download>, <button Delete>, ...]`, `querySelector('button, a')` returns the first `<button>` (Delete), not the first-in-document `<a>` (Download). (`querySelector('a, button')` returns the `<a>`; `querySelectorAll` IS document-ordered; `firstElementChild` is correct.)

Consequences: (1) In the test environment, opening a FILE row menu focuses the Delete button instead of the Download anchor — the focus-into-menu contract is violated under happy-dom. (2) The new test 'opening the menu moves focus onto the first menu item (file menu → Download <a>)' fails: `expected 'BUTTON' to be 'A'`. Real browsers are spec-compliant so production behaves correctly, but CI is red. Note the folder-menu right-click focus test passes only by coincidence — there the first child is already a `<button>`.

Fix: replace `menu.querySelector('button, a')` with a document-order-robust selection in BOTH the production code and the test. The `items` array is in scope inside `createMenu` (it is the first parameter), so `items[0]?.focus()` directly targets the first menu item in insertion order; alternatively `(menu.firstElementChild as HTMLElement | null)?.focus()` works since every direct child of the menu is an item. The test should likewise select its expected element via `menu.firstElementChild` (or `menu.children[0]`) rather than the fragile comma selector — fixing the production code alone is insufficient because the test independently re-derives its expected element with the same buggy selector and would still fail on `document.activeElement`.

issues.description: `openMenu` uses `(menu.querySelector('button, a') as HTMLElement | null)?.focus()` to move focus to the first menu item. happy-dom evaluates the comma-separated selector list in list order (not document order), so for a file-row menu this focuses the first `<button>` (Delete) instead of the first child `<a>` (Download). This causes the test-env focus-into-menu contract to be wrong and makes the new render-browse focus test fail. Replace with a document-order-robust selection: `items[0]?.focus()` (the `items` parameter is in scope) or `(menu.firstElementChild as HTMLElement | null)?.focus()`.

The newly-added test 'opening the menu moves focus onto the first menu item (file menu → Download <a>)' fails because it derives its expected element with the same fragile `menu.querySelector('button, a')` selector, which returns the first `<button>` (Delete) under happy-dom instead of the Download `<a>`. The test must select its expected first item via a document-order-robust method (e.g. `menu.firstElementChild` / `menu.children[0]`). Fixing the production code alone does not make this test pass because it independently re-derives the expected element with the same selector.

issues.description: `openMenu` uses `(menu.querySelector('button, a') as HTMLElement | null)?.focus()` to move focus to the first menu item. happy-dom evaluates the comma-separated selector list in list order (not document order), so for a file-row menu this focuses the first `<button>` (Delete) instead of the first child `<a>` (Download). This causes the test-env focus-into-menu contract to be wrong and makes the new render-browse focus test fail. Replace with a document-order-robust selection: `items[0]?.focus()` (the `items` parameter is in scope) or `(menu.firstElementChild as HTMLElement | null)?.focus()`.

The newly-added test 'opening the menu moves focus onto the first menu item (file menu → Download <a>)' fails because it derives its expected element with the same fragile `menu.querySelector('button, a')` selector, which returns the first `<button>` (Delete) under happy-dom instead of the Download `<a>`. The test must select its expected first item via a document-order-robust method (e.g. `menu.firstElementChild` / `menu.children[0]`). Fixing the production code alone does not make this test pass because it independently re-derives the expected element with the same selector.

Consequences: (1) In the test environment, opening a FILE row menu focuses the Delete button instead of the Download anchor — the focus-into-menu contract is violated under happy-dom. output.summary: Verified all prior findings against the actual working-tree state. Findings 1-4 are fully resolved: (1) the committed .bak artifact was deleted (ls confirms it is gone); (2) the dead eslint.config.js was removed leaving only eslint.config.mjs; (3) the duplicate spinner and search-wrapper describe blocks were removed from app.css.test.ts (grep confirms 1 spinner describe and 0 search-wrapper describe remain); (4) the redundant 'startApp search area events' block was removed from search-wrapper.test.ts (0 occurrences) with a clear scope comment pointing to toolbar-handlers.test.ts. The regression from my prior pass — the fragile querySelector('button, a') that broke the focus test in happy-dom — has been correctly fixed: production code now uses items[0]?.focus() and the test uses menu.firstElementChild; all 5 test files (170 tests) pass. The only remaining item is the low-severity no-op event listener in toolbar-handlers.test.ts (finding 5), which is untouched by this changeset.

findings.title: Escape stopPropagation test still registers a no-op listener with a misleading comment
findings.description: The test 'Escape key stops propagation (does not close the parent dialog)' calls parentEl.addEventListener('keydown', () => { // should NOT fire if stopPropagation worked });. The listener callback body is empty — it records nothing and asserts nothing — so it is dead code. Its comment implies the listener verifies that propagation was stopped, but the test's only real assertion is expect(stopSpy).toHaveBeenCalled(). This misleads readers into thinking the listener performs a check it does not. This was reported in the initial review and remains unaddressed (toolbar-handlers.test.ts is not modified in this changeset).
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts, in the test 'Escape key stops propagation (does not close the parent dialog)', the parentEl.addEventListener('keydown', () => { // should NOT fire if stopPropagation worked }); callback is empty and asserts nothing — it is dead, misleading code. Remove the addEventListener call entirely (the existing expect(stopSpy).toHaveBeenCalled() assertion fully covers the stopPropagation behavior). Alternatively, if you want the listener to genuinely verify non-propagation, introduce a let propagated = false; flag, set it to true inside the listener, and after dispatching the event add expect(propagated).toBe(false);. Prefer the removal option for simplicity.

output.feedback:
All three issues from attempt 1 are resolved and the fix instructions are satisfied.

1. **Menu roles added (createMenu):** `menu.setAttribute('role', 'menu')` is set on the `.row-menu` div, and every element in the `items` array gets `role='menuitem'` with the explicit skip for items that already carry a role (`if (!item.getAttribute('role'))`). This completes the ARIA menu pattern advertised by `aria-haspopup='true'`.

2. **Focus moved into menu on open:** `createMenu`'s `openMenu` now calls `items[0]?.focus()` after positioning, satisfying the aria-haspopup='menu' focus contract. As recommended in the prior feedback, this uses the `items` array (in scope as the first parameter) rather than the fragile `querySelector('button, a')` comma selector — `items[0]` is insertion-order / document-order correct, so it correctly focuses a leading `<a>` (Download) for file menus rather than the first `<button>` (Delete).

3. **Tests updated to match:** Both new focus tests now select their expected first item via `menu.firstElementChild` (document-order robust) instead of `querySelector('button, a')`. Explanatory comments reference the selector-list-order rationale so future readers don't reintroduce the bug.

4. **Unchanged as required:** `aria-haspopup='true'` and the `aria-expanded` toggling are kept exactly as-is.

**Test verification:** `src/app/dom-builders.test.ts` (23), `src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts` (23), and `src/app/render-browse.test.ts` (63) all pass — 109/109 green. The previously-failing 'opening the menu moves focus onto the first menu item (file menu → Download <a>)' test now passes.

**Code quality:** The change is minimal and targeted, the new comments are accurate and explain the non-obvious selector-ordering constraint, and no dead code or scope expansion was introduced.

output.summary: Reviewed the fixer's changes against the prior UI/UX findings. Four of the five actionable findings are fully resolved: (1) the search input now has aria-label='Search files'; (2) every decorative Bootstrap-Icons glyph (search icon, clear/close button icons, spinner icon, and all menu-item glyphs via the centralized makeIcon) now carries aria-hidden='true', with defense-in-depth catch-all tests; (3) the search spinner wrapper now has role='status' + aria-label='Loading search results'; (4) the Move/Copy prompts were rewritten to plain action-oriented copy naming the file. No functional regressions: the search debounce/Enter/Escape/clear handlers remain intact in app.ts; the removed test block was relocated (the file comment points to toolbar-handlers.test.ts); the deleted file was only a .bak backup. The menu-semantics finding is only PARTIALLY addressed: createMenu now sets role='menu'/'menuitem' and moves focus to the first item on open, but it still does not implement the arrow-key navigation (Up/Down/Home/End) that the menu pattern requires — now that role='menu' is explicitly set, screen readers will announce the dropdown as a menu and users will reach for the arrow keys, making the gap more concrete. The three prior low-severity items remain unaddressed and are reported at low for completeness.

findings.title: Menu advertises role="menu" but still lacks arrow-key item navigation (Up/Down/Home/End)
findings.description: The prior menu-semantics finding (role="menu" advertised but not implemented) was PARTIALLY fixed: createMenu now sets role="menu" on the container, role="menuitem" on each child, and moves focus onto the first item on open (items[0]?.focus()). However, the WAI-ARIA Menu Button keyboard interaction pattern also requires: ArrowDown/ArrowUp to move focus between menu items (wrapping), Home/End to jump to first/last item, and focus staying within the menu while it is open. The only keydown handler registered (onDocKey) still handles Escape alone. Because role="menu" is now explicitly set, screen readers will announce the dropdown as a menu and users will naturally reach for the arrow keys; finding them inert is a confusing expectation mismatch. (Tab/Shift+Tab still moves between items and Escape closes, so the menu is functional — just not matching the advertised pattern.) This is the remaining gap in the previously-reported medium finding.
findings.fixPrompt:
In src/app/dom-builders.ts, the `createMenu` function builds a `role="menu"` dropdown whose `onDocKey` document listener currently only closes on Escape. Complete the WAI-ARIA Menu Button keyboard pattern so the menu matches its advertised semantics:

1. Inside `onDocKey` (or a new keydown listener attached to the menu element itself when it opens and removed on close), handle ArrowDown, ArrowUp, Home, and End while the menu is open:
   - ArrowDown: move focus to the next `role="menuitem"` child (wrapping from last to first).
   - ArrowUp: move focus to the previous `role="menuitem"` child (wrapping from first to last).
   - Home: move focus to the first item.
   - End: move focus to the last item.
   Determine the currently-focused item via `document.activeElement` and the item list via the `items` array already captured in `createMenu`. Guard so these keys are only handled when the menu is open (not hidden). Call `event.preventDefault()` for the handled arrow/Home/End keys so they do not scroll the page.
2. Keep the existing Escape handling (close the menu).
3. Add focused tests in src/app/render-browse.test.ts covering: ArrowDown moves focus from the first menu item to the second; ArrowUp wraps from the first item to the last; Home focuses the first item; End focuses the last item. Reference the existing 'opening the menu moves focus onto the first menu item' test for setup patterns (menuBtnOf/actionsOf/menuOf helpers, setupCleared, file/dir fixtures).

Do not change the menu's role attributes, focus-on-open behavior, or Escape handling — only add the arrow/Home/End key handling.

findings.title: Loading spinner shown for search but not for folder navigation, creating inconsistent feedback
findings.description: Unaddressed from the prior review (and now made explicit in a code comment: 'For browse routes, no spinner is shown'). The spinner is gated behind `if (route.view === 'search')`, so navigating into a folder (a `browse` route) clears the results and fetches with no loading affordance, while searching shows a spinner for the same kind of async fetch. On slow connections this looks like a hang or a frozen listing for browse navigation. `render()` already clears `resultsEl.innerHTML` synchronously for both routes, so the browse path shows a blank panel until the fetch resolves. For consistent perceived performance, show the same spinner (or a neutral one) for browse routes too.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/render-orchestrator.ts, the `render()` function currently shows the `.search-spinner` (role="status", aria-label) only when `route.view === 'search'`. Extend the spinner to ALSO render for `route.view === 'browse'` so folder navigation shows the same loading affordance as search. Concretely: change the gate `if (route.view === 'search')` to `if (route.view === 'search' || route.view === 'browse')` (or remove the gate entirely), and update the aria-label to a neutral message that fits both routes (e.g. 'Loading…') so it is accurate for browse as well as search. Keep role="status" and aria-hidden on the icon. Update the existing 'search loading spinner' tests in src/app/render-orchestration.test.ts to reflect the new label if changed, and add an assertion (or a new test) that the spinner appears during a browse fetch. Do not change the render-race guard or the results-clearing behavior.

findings.title: Clear button has no visible focus style and is below the 24px touch-target minimum
findings.description: Unaddressed from the prior review. The `.search-wrapper .clear-btn` defines a `:hover` color change but no `:focus`/`:focus-visible` rule, so keyboard focus on the clear button relies only on the browser default outline — inconsistent with the toolbar text input (blue focus border) and the upload label (explicit :focus-within outline). The button is also only ~20px (16px glyph + padding:2px), below the 24×24 CSS px WCAG 2.5.8 minimum. Low-severity polish/consistency issue.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css, add a visible focus style for the clear button matching the app's accent and enlarge its hit area to at least 24×24 CSS px. Specifically: (1) add a `.search-wrapper .clear-btn:focus-visible` rule with an outline (e.g. `outline: 2px solid #4a9eff; outline-offset: 1px;`) consistent with the toolbar input's focus treatment; (2) increase `.search-wrapper .clear-btn`'s `padding` (currently 2px) and/or set a `min-width`/`min-height` of 24px so the tappable area meets the WCAG 2.5.8 minimum. Keep the glyph visually unchanged.

findings.title: Search placeholder and in-input icons sit just below the 4.5:1 AA contrast threshold
findings.description: Unaddressed from the prior review. The search input placeholder and overlaid search/clear icons use `#9a9a9a` on the input background `#333337`, measuring ~4.47:1 — fractionally under the 4.5:1 WCAG AA threshold for normal-size text (placeholder is 13px). The placeholder doubles as the only sighted label/affordance before typing. Low severity.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css, raise the `#9a9a9a` color used for the search placeholder and in-field icons (`.search-wrapper .search-icon`, `.search-wrapper .clear-btn`, and the placeholder via `::placeholder`) to a value that achieves at least 4.5:1 contrast against the `#333337` input background — e.g. `#a8a8a8` or lighter. Verify the change does not reduce the hover color (`#e6e6e6`) differentiation.

1. Inside `onDocKey` (or a new keydown listener attached to the menu element itself when it opens and removed on close), handle ArrowDown, ArrowUp, Home, and End while the menu is open:
   - ArrowDown: move focus to the next `role="menuitem"` child (wrapping from last to first).
   - ArrowUp: move focus to the previous `role="menuitem"` child (wrapping from first to last).
   - Home: move focus to the first item.
   - End: move focus to the last item.
   Determine the currently-focused item via `document.activeElement` and the item list via the `items` array already captured in `createMenu`. Guard so these keys are only handled when the menu is open (not hidden). Call `event.preventDefault()` for the handled arrow/Home/End keys so they do not scroll the page.
2. Keep the existing Escape handling (close the menu).
3. Add focused tests in src/app/render-browse.test.ts covering: ArrowDown moves focus from the first menu item to the second; ArrowUp wraps from the first item to the last; Home focuses the first item; End focuses the last item. Reference the existing 'opening the menu moves focus onto the first menu item' test for setup patterns (menuBtnOf/actionsOf/menuOf helpers, setupCleared, file/dir fixtures).

Do not change the menu's role attributes, focus-on-open behavior, or Escape handling — only add the arrow/Home/End key handling.

findings.title: Loading spinner shown for search but not for folder navigation, creating inconsistent feedback
findings.description: Unaddressed from the prior review (and now made explicit in a code comment: 'For browse routes, no spinner is shown'). The spinner is gated behind `if (route.view === 'search')`, so navigating into a folder (a `browse` route) clears the results and fetches with no loading affordance, while searching shows a spinner for the same kind of async fetch. On slow connections this looks like a hang or a frozen listing for browse navigation. `render()` already clears `resultsEl.innerHTML` synchronously for both routes, so the browse path shows a blank panel until the fetch resolves. For consistent perceived performance, show the same spinner (or a neutral one) for browse routes too.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/render-orchestrator.ts, the `render()` function currently shows the `.search-spinner` (role="status", aria-label) only when `route.view === 'search'`. Extend the spinner to ALSO render for `route.view === 'browse'` so folder navigation shows the same loading affordance as search. Concretely: change the gate `if (route.view === 'search')` to `if (route.view === 'search' || route.view === 'browse')` (or remove the gate entirely), and update the aria-label to a neutral message that fits both routes (e.g. 'Loading…') so it is accurate for browse as well as search. Keep role="status" and aria-hidden on the icon. Update the existing 'search loading spinner' tests in src/app/render-orchestration.test.ts to reflect the new label if changed, and add an assertion (or a new test) that the spinner appears during a browse fetch. Do not change the render-race guard or the results-clearing behavior.

findings.title: Clear button has no visible focus style and is below the 24px touch-target minimum
findings.description: Unaddressed from the prior review. The `.search-wrapper .clear-btn` defines a `:hover` color change but no `:focus`/`:focus-visible` rule, so keyboard focus on the clear button relies only on the browser default outline — inconsistent with the toolbar text input (blue focus border) and the upload label (explicit :focus-within outline). The button is also only ~20px (16px glyph + padding:2px), below the 24×24 CSS px WCAG 2.5.8 minimum. Low-severity polish/consistency issue.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css, add a visible focus style for the clear button matching the app's accent and enlarge its hit area to at least 24×24 CSS px. Specifically: (1) add a `.search-wrapper .clear-btn:focus-visible` rule with an outline (e.g. `outline: 2px solid #4a9eff; outline-offset: 1px;`) consistent with the toolbar input's focus treatment; (2) increase `.search-wrapper .clear-btn`'s `padding` (currently 2px) and/or set a `min-width`/`min-height` of 24px so the tappable area meets the WCAG 2.5.8 minimum. Keep the glyph visually unchanged.

findings.title: Search placeholder and in-input icons sit just below the 4.5:1 AA contrast threshold
findings.description: Unaddressed from the prior review. The search input placeholder and overlaid search/clear icons use `#9a9a9a` on the input background `#333337`, measuring ~4.47:1 — fractionally under the 4.5:1 WCAG AA threshold for normal-size text (placeholder is 13px). The placeholder doubles as the only sighted label/affordance before typing. Low severity.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css, raise the `#9a9a9a` color used for the search placeholder and in-field icons (`.search-wrapper .search-icon`, `.search-wrapper .clear-btn`, and the placeholder via `::placeholder`) to a value that achieves at least 4.5:1 contrast against the `#333337` input background — e.g. `#a8a8a8` or lighter. Verify the change does not reduce the hover color (`#e6e6e6`) differentiation.

output.summary: Reviewed the fixer's changes against the prior UI/UX findings. Four of the five actionable findings are fully resolved: (1) the search input now has aria-label='Search files'; (2) every decorative Bootstrap-Icons glyph (search icon, clear/close button icons, spinner icon, and all menu-item glyphs via the centralized makeIcon) now carries aria-hidden='true', with defense-in-depth catch-all tests; (3) the search spinner wrapper now has role='status' + aria-label='Loading search results'; (4) the Move/Copy prompts were rewritten to plain action-oriented copy naming the file. No functional regressions: the search debounce/Enter/Escape/clear handlers remain intact in app.ts; the removed test block was relocated (the file comment points to toolbar-handlers.test.ts); the deleted file was only a .bak backup. The menu-semantics finding is only PARTIALLY addressed: createMenu now sets role='menu'/'menuitem' and moves focus to the first item on open, but it still does not implement the arrow-key navigation (Up/Down/Home/End) that the menu pattern requires — now that role='menu' is explicitly set, screen readers will announce the dropdown as a menu and users will reach for the arrow keys, making the gap more concrete. The three prior low-severity items remain unaddressed and are reported at low for completeness.

findings.title: Menu advertises role="menu" but still lacks arrow-key item navigation (Up/Down/Home/End)
findings.description: The prior menu-semantics finding (role="menu" advertised but not implemented) was PARTIALLY fixed: createMenu now sets role="menu" on the container, role="menuitem" on each child, and moves focus onto the first item on open (items[0]?.focus()). However, the WAI-ARIA Menu Button keyboard interaction pattern also requires: ArrowDown/ArrowUp to move focus between menu items (wrapping), Home/End to jump to first/last item, and focus staying within the menu while it is open. The only keydown handler registered (onDocKey) still handles Escape alone. Because role="menu" is now explicitly set, screen readers will announce the dropdown as a menu and users will naturally reach for the arrow keys; finding them inert is a confusing expectation mismatch. (Tab/Shift+Tab still moves between items and Escape closes, so the menu is functional — just not matching the advertised pattern.) This is the remaining gap in the previously-reported medium finding.

(Tab/Shift+Tab still moves between items and Escape closes, so the menu is functional — just not matching the advertised pattern.) This is the remaining gap in the previously-reported medium finding.
findings.fixPrompt:
In src/app/dom-builders.ts, the `createMenu` function builds a `role="menu"` dropdown whose `onDocKey` document listener currently only closes on Escape. Complete the WAI-ARIA Menu Button keyboard pattern so the menu matches its advertised semantics:

1. Inside `onDocKey` (or a new keydown listener attached to the menu element itself when it opens and removed on close), handle ArrowDown, ArrowUp, Home, and End while the menu is open:
   - ArrowDown: move focus to the next `role="menuitem"` child (wrapping from last to first).
   - ArrowUp: move focus to the previous `role="menuitem"` child (wrapping from first to last).
   - Home: move focus to the first item.
   - End: move focus to the last item.
   Determine the currently-focused item via `document.activeElement` and the item list via the `items` array already captured in `createMenu`. Guard so these keys are only handled when the menu is open (not hidden). Call `event.preventDefault()` for the handled arrow/Home/End keys so they do not scroll the page.
2. Keep the existing Escape handling (close the menu).
3. Add focused tests in src/app/render-browse.test.ts covering: ArrowDown moves focus from the first menu item to the second; ArrowUp wraps from the first item to the last; Home focuses the first item; End focuses the last item. Reference the existing 'opening the menu moves focus onto the first menu item' test for setup patterns (menuBtnOf/actionsOf/menuOf helpers, setupCleared, file/dir fixtures).

Do not change the menu's role attributes, focus-on-open behavior, or Escape handling — only add the arrow/Home/End key handling.

findings.title: Loading spinner shown for search but not for folder navigation, creating inconsistent feedback
findings.description: Unaddressed from the prior review (and now made explicit in a code comment: 'For browse routes, no spinner is shown'). The spinner is gated behind `if (route.view === 'search')`, so navigating into a folder (a `browse` route) clears the results and fetches with no loading affordance, while searching shows a spinner for the same kind of async fetch. On slow connections this looks like a hang or a frozen listing for browse navigation. `render()` already clears `resultsEl.innerHTML` synchronously for both routes, so the browse path shows a blank panel until the fetch resolves. For consistent perceived performance, show the same spinner (or a neutral one) for browse routes too.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/render-orchestrator.ts, the `render()` function currently shows the `.search-spinner` (role="status", aria-label) only when `route.view === 'search'`. Extend the spinner to ALSO render for `route.view === 'browse'` so folder navigation shows the same loading affordance as search. Concretely: change the gate `if (route.view === 'search')` to `if (route.view === 'search' || route.view === 'browse')` (or remove the gate entirely), and update the aria-label to a neutral message that fits both routes (e.g. 'Loading…') so it is accurate for browse as well as search. Keep role="status" and aria-hidden on the icon. Update the existing 'search loading spinner' tests in src/app/render-orchestration.test.ts to reflect the new label if changed, and add an assertion (or a new test) that the spinner appears during a browse fetch. Do not change the render-race guard or the results-clearing behavior.

findings.title: Clear button has no visible focus style and is below the 24px touch-target minimum
findings.description: Unaddressed from the prior review. The `.search-wrapper .clear-btn` defines a `:hover` color change but no `:focus`/`:focus-visible` rule, so keyboard focus on the clear button relies only on the browser default outline — inconsistent with the toolbar text input (blue focus border) and the upload label (explicit :focus-within outline). The button is also only ~20px (16px glyph + padding:2px), below the 24×24 CSS px WCAG 2.5.8 minimum. Low-severity polish/consistency issue.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css, add a visible focus style for the clear button matching the app's accent and enlarge its hit area to at least 24×24 CSS px. Specifically: (1) add a `.search-wrapper .clear-btn:focus-visible` rule with an outline (e.g. `outline: 2px solid #4a9eff; outline-offset: 1px;`) consistent with the toolbar input's focus treatment; (2) increase `.search-wrapper .clear-btn`'s `padding` (currently 2px) and/or set a `min-width`/`min-height` of 24px so the tappable area meets the WCAG 2.5.8 minimum. Keep the glyph visually unchanged.

findings.title: Search placeholder and in-input icons sit just below the 4.5:1 AA contrast threshold
findings.description: Unaddressed from the prior review. The search input placeholder and overlaid search/clear icons use `#9a9a9a` on the input background `#333337`, measuring ~4.47:1 — fractionally under the 4.5:1 WCAG AA threshold for normal-size text (placeholder is 13px). The placeholder doubles as the only sighted label/affordance before typing. Low severity.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css, raise the `#9a9a9a` color used for the search placeholder and in-field icons (`.search-wrapper .search-icon`, `.search-wrapper .clear-btn`, and the placeholder via `::placeholder`) to a value that achieves at least 4.5:1 contrast against the `#333337` input background — e.g. `#a8a8a8` or lighter. Verify the change does not reduce the hover color (`#e6e6e6`) differentiation.

findings.title: README.md still documents removed 'Search button' and omits new instant/clear/spinner search behavior
findings.description: The fixer's diff only modified dev-diary.md; README.md was not touched at all. The branch replaced the standalone 'Search' <button> with an instant, debounced (~200 ms) search-as-you-type input that supports Enter (commit immediately), Escape (clear the query and return to browse), an in-input clear (✕) button toggled purely via CSS :placeholder-shown (no JS), and a spinner shown in the results area while a search fetch is in flight. README.md still misleads in two ways: (1) the 'Final End-to-End Verification Checklist' item 13 still reads 'Search — Search input and button filter entries recursively by name.' — a QA/reviewer following the checklist will look for a Search button that no longer exists; (2) the README has detailed behavior sections (Limits, File name handling, Symlinks, Deep Linking) but nothing describing the search UX, so users reading the docs won't discover the instant/clear/spinner/Escape behaviors. The README must be corrected so its checklist matches the current UI and a concise section documents the new search experience.
findings.fixPrompt:
Edit README.md in two places to make it match the current search UX.

1) Update the checklist. In README.md, change the line numbered 13 under '## Final End-to-End Verification Checklist' from:

13. **Search** — Search input and button filter entries recursively by name.

to:

13. **Search** — The search input filters entries recursively by name with instant, debounced (200 ms) search-as-you-type; pressing Enter submits immediately, pressing Escape clears the query and returns to browse, the in-input ✕ clear button resets the query, and a spinner shows in the results area while a search fetch is in flight.

2) Add a new behavior section. Insert a new '## Search' section immediately BEFORE the existing '## Deep Linking' section (i.e. right after the '## Symlinks and junctions' section) with the following content (preserve the existing section ordering otherwise):

## Search

Search is instant and debounced. As you type in the search box, a query fires ~200 ms after you stop typing — no separate Search button to click. Enter submits the current query immediately. Escape clears the query and returns to browse. When the input has text, a clear (✕) button appears inside it; click it (or clear the input) to reset the query. While a search request is in flight, a spinner is shown in the results area.

Do not modify any other section. Verify the markdown still renders (matching heading levels, blank lines around the new section).

findings.title: README.md still documents removed 'Search button' and omits new instant/clear/spinner search behavior
findings.description: The fixer's diff only modified dev-diary.md; README.md was not touched at all. The branch replaced the standalone 'Search' <button> with an instant, debounced (~200 ms) search-as-you-type input that supports Enter (commit immediately), Escape (clear the query and return to browse), an in-input clear (✕) button toggled purely via CSS :placeholder-shown (no JS), and a spinner shown in the results area while a search fetch is in flight. README.md still misleads in two ways: (1) the 'Final End-to-End Verification Checklist' item 13 still reads 'Search — Search input and button filter entries recursively by name.' — a QA/reviewer following the checklist will look for a Search button that no longer exists; (2) the README has detailed behavior sections (Limits, File name handling, Symlinks, Deep Linking) but nothing describing the search UX, so users reading the docs won't discover the instant/clear/spinner/Escape behaviors. The README must be corrected so its checklist matches the current UI and a concise section documents the new search experience.
findings.fixPrompt:
Edit README.md in two places to make it match the current search UX.

1) Update the checklist. In README.md, change the line numbered 13 under '## Final End-to-End Verification Checklist' from:

13. **Search** — Search input and button filter entries recursively by name.

to:

13. **Search** — The search input filters entries recursively by name with instant, debounced (200 ms) search-as-you-type; pressing Enter submits immediately, pressing Escape clears the query and returns to browse, the in-input ✕ clear button resets the query, and a spinner shows in the results area while a search fetch is in flight.

2) Add a new behavior section. Insert a new '## Search' section immediately BEFORE the existing '## Deep Linking' section (i.e. right after the '## Symlinks and junctions' section) with the following content (preserve the existing section ordering otherwise):

## Search

Search is instant and debounced. As you type in the search box, a query fires ~200 ms after you stop typing — no separate Search button to click. Enter submits the current query immediately. Escape clears the query and returns to browse. When the input has text, a clear (✕) button appears inside it; click it (or clear the input) to reset the query. While a search request is in flight, a spinner is shown in the results area.

Do not modify any other section. Verify the markdown still renders (matching heading levels, blank lines around the new section).

Re-reporting that finding as still outstanding.

issues.description: The createMenu function's onDocKey handler (line ~153) only handles Escape. output.feedback: The tests in render-browse.test.ts are well-written, correctly structured, and follow all existing project patterns — but they all fail because the implementation was never added to dom-builders.ts. This is the third review attempt with the same root cause: the createMenu function's onDocKey handler (src/app/dom-builders.ts:~153) still only handles Escape. No ArrowDown, ArrowUp, Home, or End handling exists. The 5 navigation tests (ArrowDown first→second, ArrowUp wraps first→last, Home last→first, End first→last, full ArrowDown round-trip) all fail because document.activeElement remains on items[0] after every keypress. The Escape regression test passes (it was already working). To fix: add ArrowDown/ArrowUp/Home/End handling to onDocKey inside createMenu, using the items array already in closure scope, guarded by !menu.hidden, with preventDefault() for each handled key. The existing tests will then pass without modification.
